Chapter 01 Historical Background — 1773–1947
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Historical Background of the
Indian Constitution
📖 Chapter 01
📅 1773 – 1947
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Quick Revision  ●  MCQ-Ready  ●  Exam Traps  ●  First-Ever Facts  ●  3-Page Compact
🏛️ PART A : COMPANY RULE PERIOD (1773 – 1857) 5 Key Acts
📅 ACT / YEAR 🏷 NICKNAME 🔑 KEY HIGHLIGHTS
1773
Regulating Act
1st Parliament
Control of EIC
  • ⭐ KEY GG of Bengal created → 1st GG: Warren Hastings
  • Executive Council of GG established (4 members)
  • Supreme Court at Fort William, Calcutta (1774) — 1 CJ + 3 Judges
  • Subordinated Governors of Bombay & Madras to Bengal
  • Court of Directors required to report revenue to British Govt.
1784
Pitt's India Act
🍞 Half-Loaf
System
  • ⭐ KEY Dual Control: Court of Directors (commerce) + Board of Control (politics)
  • GG Council reduced to 3 members
  • Company territories = "British Possession of India"
  • Governor's councils in Madras & Bombay created
1813
Charter Act
EIC Monopoly
Broken
  • EIC monopoly TERMINATED (except tea & China trade)
  • Trade with India opened to all British subjects
  • Christian missionaries allowed; grants for education
1833
Charter Act
⛪ Saint Helena
Act
  • ⭐ KEY GGI created → 1st GGI: Lord William Bentinck
  • EIC became purely administrative body (no commercial role)
  • 🔴 SLAVERY ABOLISHED in India
  • Law Commission → 1st member: T.B. Macaulay
  • EIC monopoly on China trade ended
1853
Charter Act
📜 Parliamentary
System Begins
  • Legislative & Executive functions of GG Council SEPARATED
  • 6 new members added to Central Legislative Council
  • ⭐ KEY Open competition for Civil Services (ICS)
  • 1st Indian ICS: Satyendranath Tagore
👑 PART B : CROWN RULE PERIOD (1858 – 1947) 7 Key Acts
📅 ACT / YEAR 🏷 NICKNAME 🔑 KEY HIGHLIGHTS
1858
GoI Act
👸 Queen's
Proclamation
  • ⭐ KEY EIC ABOLISHED → Crown Rule begins
  • Secretary of State for India + Council of India (15 members)
  • GGI became Viceroy → 1st Viceroy: Lord Canning
  • Board of Control & Court of Directors abolished
1861
IC Act
🤝 Indians
Nominated
  • Indians nominated as non-official members to councils
  • Statutory recognition to Portfolio System (Lord Canning)
  • High Courts in Calcutta, Madras & Bombay established
  • Process of Decentralisation began
1892
IC Act
🗳 Indirect
Elections
  • Introduced indirect elections (nomination system)
  • Councils could discuss Budget & ask questions to Executive
1909
IC Act
⚖ Morley-Minto
Reforms
  • ⭐ KEY 1st time DIRECT ELECTIONS actually established
  • Separate electorate for Muslims → Minto = Father of Communal Electorate
  • Imperial Legislative Council: 16 → 60 members
  • 1st Indian in Viceroy's Council: Satyendra Prasad Sinha (Law Member)
1919
GoI Act
🔄 Montague-
Chelmsford
  • ⭐ KEY DYARCHY in Provinces: Transferred (Ministers) vs Reserved (Governor)
  • 1st BICAMERALISM at Centre: Leg. Assembly (140) + Leg. Council (60)
  • 1st Direct Elections in India (countrywide)
  • Provincial budget separated from Central budget
  • Separate electorate extended to Sikhs, Christians, Anglo-Indians, Europeans
1935
GoI Act
📜 Blueprint of
Constitution
  • ⭐ KEY 3 LISTS: Federal (59) | Provincial (54) | Concurrent (36)
  • Dyarchy abolished in provinces → Provincial Autonomy
  • Dyarchy introduced at Centre
  • Bicameralism in 6 provinces: Assam, Bengal, Bombay, Bihar, Madras, UP
  • Federal Court (1937) + RBI established
  • Residuary Powers → Viceroy (Governor-General)
1947
Independence Act
🇮🇳 Mountbatten
Plan
  • ⭐ KEY India declared INDEPENDENT & SOVEREIGN STATE
  • Viceroy → Constitutional Head | Constituent Assembly = Legislative body
  • British PM: Clement Attlee | 1st GG: Lord Mountbatten
  • 1st & Last Indian GG: C. Rajagopalachari
⚙️ DYARCHY SYSTEM (1919) – Easy Comparison
⚙️ HOW DYARCHY WORKED IN PROVINCES — Montague-Chelmsford Reforms 1919

✅ TRANSFERRED SUBJECTS

  • Education & Health
  • Agriculture & Public Works
  • Local Self-Government

👨‍⚖️ Administered by Minister → Responsible to Legislative Council

VS

⛔ RESERVED SUBJECTS

  • Law & Order
  • Land Revenue & Finance
  • Irrigation & Military

🏛 Administered by Governor → NOT responsible to Council

⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗GG of Bengal ≠ GG of India
Warren Hastings ≠ Lord Bentinck
✗1st Viceroy = Lord Canning
1st GGI = Lord Wm. Bentinck
✗Dyarchy → Provinces: 1919
Dyarchy → Centre: 1935
✗Direct elections established 1909
Direct elections in India → 1919
✗RBI → GoI Act 1935
NOT the Independence Act 1947
✅ RAPID RECALL
★1773 → 1st Parliamentary control of EIC
★1784 → Dual Control (Half-Loaf System)
★1833 → GGI created; Slavery abolished
★1853 → Open civil services; ICS begins
★1858 → Crown Rule; Viceroy created
★1909 → Direct elections + Communal electorate
★1919 → Dyarchy + Bicameralism at Centre
★1935 → 3 Lists; RBI; Provincial Autonomy
★1947 → Independent & Sovereign India 🇮🇳
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st GG of Bengal → Warren Hastings
➤1st GGI → Lord William Bentinck
➤1st Viceroy → Lord Canning
➤1st & Last Indian GG → C. Rajagopalachari
➤1st Indian ICS → Satyendranath Tagore
➤1st Indian in Viceroy's Council → S.P. Sinha
➤Father of Communal Electorate → Lord Minto
➤1st Law Commission member → T.B. Macaulay
➤Supreme Court at Calcutta → 1774
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Which Act made Warren Hastings the Governor-General of Bengal?
Regulating Act, 1773
Q2
The "Half-Loaf System" is associated with which Act?
Pitt's India Act, 1784
Q3
Slavery was abolished in India by which Act?
Charter Act, 1833
Q4
Who was the first Indian to join the Indian Civil Services?
Satyendranath Tagore
Q5
Who is known as the Father of Communal Electorate?
Lord Minto (1909)
Q6
Dyarchy was introduced at the provincial level by which Act?
GoI Act, 1919
Q7
Which Act established the Reserve Bank of India?
GoI Act, 1935
Q8
The "Mountbatten Plan" is related to which legislation?
Independence Act, 1947
Q9
Who was the first and last Indian Governor-General of India?
C. Rajagopalachari
Q10
Bicameralism was first introduced at the Centre by which Act?
GoI Act, 1919
Q11
Who was the first Viceroy of India?
Lord Canning (1858)
Q12
In which year was the Supreme Court established at Calcutta?
1774
Q13
Who was the first Governor-General of India (GGI)?
Lord William Bentinck
Q14
Who was the first Indian Law Member in the Viceroy's Council?
S.P. Sinha (1909)
Q15
Which Act introduced the term "British Possession of India"?
Pitt's India Act, 1784
Q16
Who was the first member of the Law Commission of India?
T.B. Macaulay (1833)
Q17
Which Act separated provincial budget from the central budget?
GoI Act, 1919
Q18
The 3 Lists — Federal, Provincial, Concurrent — were created by?
GoI Act, 1935
Q19
In which year was the Federal Court of India established?
1937 (GoI Act, 1935)
Q20
Who was the British Prime Minister at the time of Indian Independence?
Clement Attlee
Q21
Which Act nominated Indians to councils for the first time?
IC Act, 1861
Q22
Which Act gave statutory recognition to the Portfolio System?
IC Act, 1861
Q23
Indirect elections were first introduced in India by which Act?
IC Act, 1892
Q24
Imperial Legislative Council was expanded from 16 to 60 by?
IC Act, 1909
Q25
Which Act introduced Provincial Autonomy in India?
GoI Act, 1935
Q26
The process of Decentralisation in India began with which Act?
IC Act, 1861
Q27
EIC's trade monopoly over India was terminated by which Act?
Charter Act, 1813
Q28
Which Act made the EIC a purely administrative body?
Charter Act, 1833
Q29
Board of Control & Court of Directors were abolished by?
GoI Act, 1858
Q30
High Courts in Calcutta, Madras & Bombay were established by?
IC Act, 1861
Q31
Which Act ended EIC rule and began Crown Rule in India?
GoI Act, 1858
Q32
Separate electorate extended to Sikhs & Christians by which Act?
GoI Act, 1919
Q33
Residuary Powers under GoI Act 1935 were vested with whom?
Viceroy (GG)
Q34
Bicameralism in 6 provinces was introduced by which Act?
GoI Act, 1935
Q35
Constituent Assembly was given dual functions by which Act?
Independence Act, 1947
Q36
"Saint Helena Act" is another name for which legislation?
Charter Act, 1833
Q37
"Queen's Proclamation" is associated with which Act?
GoI Act, 1858
Q38
Which Act first introduced direct elections in India?
IC Act, 1909
Q39
Dyarchy at the Centre was introduced by which Act?
GoI Act, 1935
Q40
Which Act is considered the blueprint of the Indian Constitution?
GoI Act, 1935

Chapter 02 Making of Constitution — 1934–1950
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Making of the
Indian Constitution
📖 Chapter 02
📅 1934 – 1950
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Quick Revision  ●  MCQ-Ready  ●  Exam Traps  ●  Key Committees  ●  Sources of Constitution
📅 PART A : DEMAND FOR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY — Timeline Key Years
YEAREVENTKEY DETAILS
1928
Nehru Report
  • ⭐ KEY First draft of Indian Constitution — All-Parties Conference, Lucknow
  • Chaired by Moti Lal Nehru
1934
Idea of CA
  • ⭐ KEY M.N. Roy — First person to propose the idea of a Constituent Assembly
1935
INC Demand
  • Indian National Congress officially demanded a CA for the first time
1938
Nehru's Declaration
  • Jawaharlal Nehru declared Constitution must be framed by a CA elected on adult franchise, free from external interference
1940
August Offer
  • August Offer — British accepted the demand for a Constituent Assembly
1942
Cripps Mission
  • Sir Stafford Cripps proposed granting India dominion status + establishing CA after WWII
1945
Wavell Plan
  • Failed — led to breakdown of the Simla Conference
1946
Cabinet Mission
  • ⭐ KEY Members: Lord Pethick-Lawrence (Chairman), Sir Stafford Cripps, A.V. Alexander
  • Accepted by all parties initially → Rejected by Muslim League
  • CA constituted under Cabinet Mission Plan in November 1946
🏛️ PART B : CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY — Composition & Key Facts Must Know
📊 Membership Breakdown
389
Total Members originally
292
Elected by Provincial Legislative Assemblies
93
Nominated by Princely States (stayed away)
4
Chief Commissioner Provinces (Delhi, Ajmer-Marwar, Coorg, British Baluchistan)
284
Members who actually signed the Constitution (incl. 15 women)
🗳️ Election Results (July–Aug 1946)
208
Indian National Congress
73
Muslim League
15
Small Groups & Independents
93
Princely States (not filled – stayed away)

⚠️ CA met first on 9th Dec 1946 at Constitution Hall (now Central Hall of Parliament House), New Delhi

⚙️ PART C : WORKING OF THE CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY Sessions & Timeline
TOPICKEY FACTS
Objective Resolution
  • ⭐ KEY Introduced by Jawaharlal Nehru on Dec 13, 1946 → Adopted on Jan 22, 1947
  • Basis of the Preamble — declared India a Republic with political, social & economic democracy
  • Guaranteed Fundamental Rights; State to safeguard minority & backward class rights
Sessions & Duration
  • 11 sessions held over 165 days
  • Total duration: ⭐ KEY 2 years, 11 months & 18 days
  • Total cost: ₹64 lakhs
  • 1st session: 9–23 Dec 1946  |  10th: 6–17 Oct 1949  |  11th: 14–26 Nov 1949
Chairmanship
  • As Constitutional body → Chaired by Dr. Rajendra Prasad
  • As Legislative body → Chaired by G.V. Mavalankar (1st Speaker of Lok Sabha)
Enactment & Enforcement
  • Adopted in 1949 — Original: 395 Articles & 8 Schedules | Currently: 470 Articles & 12 Schedules
  • Some Articles (5,6,7,8,9,60,324…) came into force on Nov 26, 1949 — Constitution Day
  • ⭐ KEY Major part enforced on Jan 26, 1950 — Republic Day
  • Jan 26 chosen to honour Purna Swaraj Day (Jan 26, 1930) — Lahore Session of INC
📋 PART D : COMMITTEES OF THE CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY 22 Committees
MAJOR COMMITTEE (8)HEADED BY
Union Powers CommitteeJ.L. Nehru
Union Constitution CommitteeJ.L. Nehru
States CommitteeJ.L. Nehru
Rules of Procedure CommitteeDr. Rajendra Prasad
Steering CommitteeDr. Rajendra Prasad
Provincial Constitution CommitteeSardar Patel
Advisory Committee (FR, Minorities, Tribal)Sardar Patel
⭐ Drafting CommitteeDr. B.R. Ambedkar
MINOR COMMITTEES (13 — Select)HEADED BY
Finance and Staff CommitteeDr. Rajendra Prasad
Credentials CommitteeA.K. Ayyar
House CommitteeB. Pattabhi Sitaramayya
Order of Business CommitteeDr. K.M. Munshi
Ad-hoc Committee on National FlagDr. Rajendra Prasad
Committee on Functions of CAG.V. Mavalankar
Ad-hoc Committee on Supreme CourtS. Varadachari
Linguistic Provinces CommissionS.K. Dar
🌍 PART E : SOURCES OF INDIAN CONSTITUTION — 10 Countries Most Asked!
COUNTRYFEATURES BORROWED
🇺🇸 USA
  • ⭐ KEY Supreme Court, Preamble, Written Constitution, Judicial Review, Vice President, Fundamental Rights, Impeachment of Judges
🇬🇧 UK
  • Single Citizenship, Rule of Law, Cabinet System, CAG, Bicameralism, Parliamentary Govt, Prerogative Writs, Law making procedure
🇨🇦 Canada
  • Quasi-Federal Form, Distribution of Powers, Residual Powers with Centre, Advisory Jurisdiction of SC
🇮🇪 Ireland
  • Election of President, Nomination to Rajya Sabha, DPSP
🇫🇷 France
  • Republic ideals — Liberty, Equality & Fraternity in Preamble
📜 GoI Act 1935
  • ⭐ KEY Federal Scheme, Provincial Autonomy, Public Service Commissions, Office of Governor, Judiciary, Administrative Details
🇿🇦 S. Africa
  • Procedure for Amendment of Constitution, Election of Rajya Sabha Members
🇩🇪 Germany
  • Emergency Provisions, Suspension of Fundamental Rights during Emergency
🇷🇺 USSR
  • Fundamental Duties, Five Year Plans, Social, Economic & Political Justice in Preamble
🇦🇺 Australia
  • Freedom of Trade & Commerce, Concurrent List, Language of Preamble, Joint Sitting of LS & RS
🇯🇵 Japan
  • Procedure Established by Law
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗M.N. Roy → 1st to propose CA idea (1934)
INC demanded CA → 1935
✗Mahatma Gandhi was NOT a member of CA
✗Nov 26, 1949 → Constitution Day
Jan 26, 1950 → Republic Day
✗Drafting Committee Chair → Ambedkar
CA Chair → Dr. Rajendra Prasad
✗Original: 395 Articles, 8 Schedules
Current: 470 Articles, 12 Schedules
✗DPSP → Ireland (NOT UK or USA)
✗Fundamental Duties → USSR
✅ RAPID RECALL
★1934 → M.N. Roy: 1st proposed CA idea
★1946 → Cabinet Mission Plan set up CA
★9 Dec 1946 → CA first meeting
★13 Dec 1946 → Objective Resolution introduced
★22 Jan 1947 → Objective Resolution adopted
★26 Nov 1949 → Constitution adopted
★26 Jan 1950 → Constitution enforced
★11 sessions, 165 days, ₹64 lakhs
★Duration: 2 yrs, 11 months, 18 days
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st to propose CA → M.N. Roy (1934)
➤1st INC CA demand → 1935
➤CA Chairman → Dr. Rajendra Prasad
➤Drafting Committee Chair → Dr. B.R. Ambedkar
➤Legal Advisor to CA → B.N. Rau
➤Chief Draftsman → S.N. Mukherjee
➤English calligrapher → Prem Behari Narain Raizada
➤Hindi calligrapher → Vasant Krishan Vaidya
➤1st Speaker of Lok Sabha → G.V. Mavalankar
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Who first put forward the idea of a Constituent Assembly for India?
M.N. Roy (1934)
Q2
When did the Indian National Congress first demand a Constituent Assembly?
1935
Q3
Which plan/offer first accepted the demand for a Constituent Assembly?
August Offer, 1940
Q4
Who was the Chairman of the Cabinet Mission Plan (1946)?
Lord Pethick-Lawrence
Q5
When did the Constituent Assembly meet for the first time?
9th December 1946
Q6
Where did the Constituent Assembly meet for the first time?
Constitution Hall, New Delhi
Q7
What was the total strength of the Constituent Assembly?
389 Members
Q8
How many members signed the Indian Constitution?
284 Members
Q9
Who introduced the Objective Resolution in the CA?
Jawaharlal Nehru
Q10
When was the Objective Resolution adopted by the CA?
22nd January 1947
Q11
How many sessions did the Constituent Assembly hold?
11 Sessions (165 days)
Q12
How long did it take to frame the Indian Constitution?
2 yrs, 11 months, 18 days
Q13
What was the total cost of making the Indian Constitution?
₹64 Lakhs
Q14
Who was the Chairman of the Constituent Assembly?
Dr. Rajendra Prasad
Q15
Who chaired the CA when working as a legislative body?
G.V. Mavalankar
Q16
Who was the Chairman of the Drafting Committee?
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar
Q17
Who was the Legal/Constitutional Advisor to the CA?
B.N. Rau
Q18
Who was the Chief Draftsman of the Constitution in the CA?
S.N. Mukherjee
Q19
Who calligraphed the English version of the Constitution?
Prem Behari Narain Raizada
Q20
Who calligraphed the Hindi version of the Constitution?
Vasant Krishan Vaidya
Q21
Who designed the borders on every page of the Constitution?
Nand Lal Bose
Q22
What was the symbol/emblem of the Constituent Assembly?
The Elephant
Q23
On which date was the Constitution of India adopted?
26th November 1949
Q24
On which date did the Constitution of India come into force?
26th January 1950
Q25
Why was Jan 26 chosen as Republic Day?
To honour Purna Swaraj Day 1930
Q26
How many Articles and Schedules did the original Constitution have?
395 Articles, 8 Schedules
Q27
How many Articles and Schedules does the Constitution have currently?
470 Articles, 12 Schedules
Q28
Fundamental Rights in the Indian Constitution were borrowed from?
USA
Q29
DPSP (Directive Principles of State Policy) borrowed from?
Ireland
Q30
Fundamental Duties in the Constitution were borrowed from?
USSR
Q31
Emergency Provisions in the Constitution were borrowed from?
Germany (Weimar)
Q32
Cabinet System and Rule of Law were borrowed from?
United Kingdom (UK)
Q33
Residual Powers with Centre concept was borrowed from?
Canada
Q34
Concurrent List concept was borrowed from?
Australia
Q35
Amendment procedure of Constitution was borrowed from?
South Africa
Q36
Liberty, Equality & Fraternity in Preamble were borrowed from?
France
Q37
How many committees did the CA form?
22 Committees
Q38
Who headed the Union Powers Committee of the CA?
J.L. Nehru
Q39
Was Mahatma Gandhi a member of the Constituent Assembly?
No — NOT a member
Q40
Who was the Secretary of the Constituent Assembly?
H.V. Iyengar

Chapter 03 Preamble & Composition — SSDDR + Basic Structure
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Preamble & Composition of the
Indian Constitution
📖 Chapter 03
🇮🇳 SOVEREIGN SOCIALIST SECULAR
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Preamble  ●  SSDDR  ●  Objectives  ●  Landmark Cases  ●  Basic Structure  ●  40 MCQs
📜 PART A : THE PREAMBLE — Key Dates & Famous Quotes Must Know
🇮🇳 The Preamble of India
WE, THE PEOPLE OF INDIA, having solemnly resolved to constitute India into a
SOVEREIGN SOCIALIST SECULAR DEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC
and to secure to all its citizens:
JUSTICE — Social, Economic and Political  |  LIBERTY — of Thought, Expression, Belief, Faith and Worship
EQUALITY — of Status and of Opportunity  |  FRATERNITY — assuring Dignity of the Individual and Unity & Integrity of the Nation
IN OUR CONSTITUENT ASSEMBLY this twenty-sixth day of November, 1949
📅 KEY DATEEVENT
13 Dec 1946Objective Resolution introduced by J.L. Nehru
22 Jan 1947Objective Resolution adopted by CA
26 Nov 1949Constitution adopted — Constitution Day (Samvidhan Diwas, since 2015)
26 Jan 1950Constitution enforced — Republic Day
🎓 SCHOLARFAMOUS QUOTE ON PREAMBLE
N.A. Palkhivala"ID Card of the Constitution"
K.M. Munshi"Political Horoscope of the Constitution"
Pt. Thakur Das"Jewel Set of the Constitution"
K.C. WheareIndia = "Quasi-Federal" State
Granville Austin"Cooperative Federalism"
Morris Jones"Bargaining Federalism"
🏛️ PART B : NATURE OF INDIAN STATE — S S D D R ⭐ Most Asked!
🇮🇳 SOVEREIGN
People have supreme power internally & externally
No external power can dictate India
Added: Original 1949
🤝 SOCIALIST
Wealth shared equitably
Govt regulates ownership to reduce inequality
Added: 42nd Amendment 1976
India = Mixed Economy
🕌 SECULAR
No official state religion
Freedom to follow any religion
Added: 42nd Amendment 1976
India = Positive Secularism
🗳️ DEMOCRATIC
Govt of the People, By the People, For the People
India = Indirect/Representative Democracy
Switzerland = Direct Democracy
🏅 REPUBLIC
Head of State elected by people
Not hereditary succession
UK is NOT a Republic
Added: Original 1949
⚠️ TRICK TO REMEMBER — S S D D R = Sovereign Socialist Secular Democratic Republic | Socialist & Secular added by 42nd Amendment, 1976 (Indira Gandhi)
🎯 PART C : FOUR OBJECTIVES OF THE PREAMBLE — JLEF 4 Key Terms
⚖️ JUSTICE — Social, Economic & Political
No discrimination on caste, religion, gender. Social inequalities must end.
Ideals of Justice borrowed from Russia (USSR)
3 Types: Social | Economic | Political
🕊️ LIBERTY — Thought, Expression, Belief, Faith & Worship
No unreasonable restrictions on citizens' thoughts or expressions.
Liberty ideals borrowed from France
5 Types: Thought | Expression | Belief | Faith | Worship
🤝 EQUALITY — of Status & Opportunity
All equal before law. Government ensures equal opportunities for all.
Traditional social inequalities must end.
2 Types: Status | Opportunity
💜 FRATERNITY — Dignity of Individual + Unity & Integrity
All citizens as one family. No citizen should treat another as inferior.
Ensures dignity of the individual AND unity & integrity of the Nation.
Borrowed from: France
⚖️ PART D : IS PREAMBLE PART OF CONSTITUTION? — Landmark Cases ⭐ Very Important!
CASEYEARRULING
Berubari Union Case 1960
  • Preamble is NOT a part of the Constitution
  • Cannot be amended | Only a key to understanding the Constitution
  • Not legally enforceable
Kesavananda Bharati Case 1973
  • ⭐ KEY OVERRULED Berubari Case — Preamble IS a part of Constitution
  • Reflects the BASIC STRUCTURE of the Constitution
  • CAN be amended — subject to basic structure doctrine
LIC of India Case 1995
  • Reaffirmed Kesavananda Bharati ruling
  • Preamble is part of Constitution and reflects its basic structure
🏗️ PART E : STRUCTURE OF CONSTITUTION + BASIC STRUCTURE DOCTRINE
📊 Constitution at a Glance
PARTS
22
Original 1949
25
Current
ARTICLES
395
Original 1949
470+
Current
SCHEDULES
8
Original 1949
12
Current
🔒 Basic Structure Doctrine (Kesavananda Bharati, 1973)
🔒Federal & Secular character
🔒Democratic governance
🔒Sovereignty of India
🔒Liberty of thought & expression
🔒Welfare State principle
🔒Equality of status & opportunity
🔒Separation of powers
🔒Dignity of the individual
🔒Unity & Integrity of the nation
⚠️Parliament CANNOT amend these!
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗Preamble NOT part of Constitution → Berubari 1960
IS part → Kesavananda 1973
✗Socialist & Secular → 42nd Amendment 1976
NOT in original Preamble 1949
✗India = Positive Secularism (involved)
West = Strict separation of Church & State
✗India = Indirect Democracy
Switzerland = Direct Democracy
✗Justice borrowed from USSR/Russia
Liberty & Fraternity from France
✗UK is NOT a Republic State
India IS a Republic (elected head)
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Preamble = "ID Card" (Palkhivala)
★Preamble compiled AFTER full Constitution was drafted
★SOVEREIGN + DEMOCRATIC + REPUBLIC → Original 1949
★SOCIALIST + SECULAR → Added by 42nd Amendment 1976
★Justice = 3 types | Liberty = 5 types | Equality = 2 types
★Basic Structure Doctrine → Kesavananda Bharati 1973
★Preamble = modified Objective Resolution (J.L. Nehru)
★India = Quasi-Federal (K.C. Wheare)
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤Objective Resolution introduced → 13 Dec 1946
➤Objective Resolution adopted → 22 Jan 1947
➤Constitution Day celebrated → since 2015
➤"Political Horoscope" → K.M. Munshi
➤"Jewel Set" → Pt. Thakur Das
➤Original Parts: 22 | Current: 25
➤Original Articles: 395 | Current: 470+
➤Original Schedules: 8 | Current: 12
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
What is the Preamble described as by N.A. Palkhivala?
ID Card of the Constitution
Q2
Who called the Preamble the "Political Horoscope of the Constitution"?
K.M. Munshi
Q3
Who described the Preamble as the "Jewel Set of the Constitution"?
Pt. Thakur Das
Q4
When was the Objective Resolution introduced by Nehru?
13th December 1946
Q5
When was the Objective Resolution adopted by the CA?
22nd January 1947
Q6
When was the Constitution of India adopted?
26th November 1949
Q7
When did the Constitution of India come into force?
26th January 1950
Q8
Constitution Day (Samvidhan Diwas) has been celebrated since which year?
2015
Q9
Which two words were added to the Preamble by the 42nd Amendment?
Socialist & Secular (1976)
Q10
Which words were present in the original Preamble of 1949?
Sovereign, Democratic, Republic
Q11
India follows which type of secularism?
Positive Secularism
Q12
India follows which type of democracy?
Indirect (Representative) Democracy
Q13
Which country follows Direct Democracy?
Switzerland
Q14
Which case held that the Preamble is NOT part of the Constitution?
Berubari Union Case, 1960
Q15
Which case ruled that the Preamble IS part of the Constitution?
Kesavananda Bharati Case, 1973
Q16
Which case reaffirmed that the Preamble reflects the Basic Structure?
LIC of India Case, 1995
Q17
When was the Basic Structure Doctrine established?
Kesavananda Bharati Case, 1973
Q18
How many types of Justice are mentioned in the Preamble?
3 — Social, Economic, Political
Q19
How many forms of Liberty are mentioned in the Preamble?
5 — Thought, Expression, Belief, Faith, Worship
Q20
How many types of Equality are mentioned in the Preamble?
2 — Status & Opportunity
Q21
Ideals of Justice in the Preamble were borrowed from?
Russia (USSR)
Q22
Liberty, Equality & Fraternity were borrowed from?
France
Q23
Who described India's federal system as "Quasi-Federal"?
K.C. Wheare
Q24
Who called India's federation "Cooperative Federalism"?
Granville Austin
Q25
Who described India's federation as "Bargaining Federalism"?
Morris Jones
Q26
How many Parts did the original Constitution have?
22 Parts
Q27
How many Parts does the current Constitution have?
25 Parts
Q28
How many Articles did the original Constitution have?
395 Articles
Q29
How many Schedules does the current Constitution have?
12 Schedules
Q30
Was the Preamble compiled before or after the Constitution?
AFTER the full Constitution
Q31
Can the Preamble be amended by Parliament?
Yes — subject to Basic Structure
Q32
The Preamble is a modified version of which resolution?
Objective Resolution by J.L. Nehru
Q33
Which amendment added "Socialist" and "Secular" to the Preamble?
42nd Constitutional Amendment, 1976
Q34
What does "Republic" mean in the Preamble?
Elected Head of State (not hereditary)
Q35
Is the UK a Republic State?
No — Monarch is Head of State
Q36
Which country follows a Capitalistic economic model?
USA
Q37
India follows which type of economic system?
Mixed Economy (Socialist)
Q38
India does NOT follow which doctrine strictly as per the Constitution?
Strict Separation of Powers
Q39
Fraternity ensures dignity of the individual and what else?
Unity & Integrity of the Nation
Q40
Parliament CANNOT amend features protected by which doctrine?
Basic Structure Doctrine (1973)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 03 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 03 of 14
Chapter 04 Territories & Citizenship — Arts. 1–11
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Territories & Citizenship of
India — Articles 1–11
📖 Chapter 04
🗺️ Union of States
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Articles 1–4  ●  State Reorganisation  ●  Article 371  ●  Citizenship Act 1955  ●  CAA 2019  ●  OCI/PIO/NRI
🗺️ PART A : UNION & ITS TERRITORIES — Articles 1 to 4 Schedule 1 & 4
ARTICLESUBJECT MATTER
Art. 1
  • India, that is Bharat, shall be a Union of States
  • Names of States/UTs in First Schedule
Art. 2
  • Admission or establishment of new states by Parliament
Art. 3
  • Formation of new states & alteration of areas, boundaries, or names of existing states
  • Prior permission of President required (5th CAA, 1955)
Art. 4
  • Laws under Arts. 2 & 3 amend the 1st Schedule (States) & 4th Schedule (Rajya Sabha seats)
  • NOT treated as constitutional amendments under Art. 368
⚙️ Key Procedure under Article 3
✅President's prior permission required before introducing bill
✅Bill introduced in either House of Parliament
⚠️State Legislature's opinion sought but NOT binding on Parliament
✅Must be a Government Bill introduced by a Cabinet Minister
✅Passed by Simple Majority (not special majority)
💡 India = Union of States (NOT Federation)
States CANNOT secede from the Union
🕐 PART B : REORGANISATION OF STATES — Historical Timeline Commissions
1948
Dhar Committee → Recommended AGAINST reorganisation on language basis
1948
JVP
JVP Committee → Members: Jawaharlal Nehru, Vallabhbhai Patel, Pattabhi Sitaramayya
Also AGAINST linguistic basis for reorganisation
1 Oct
1953
⭐ KEY Andhra State formed — 1st state formed on linguistic basis
Led by Potti Sriramulu (died after 56-day hunger strike)
1953–55
Fazl Ali Commission → Members: Fazl Ali, K.M. Panikkar, H.N. Kunzru
Rejected "One Language — One State" theory
1 Nov
1956
⭐ KEY States Reorganisation Act 1956 → Abolished Part A, B, C, D classification
Created 14 States & 6 Union Territories
🔒 PART C : SPECIAL PROVISIONS — Article 371 (A to J) Part XXI
ARTICLESTATESPECIAL PROTECTION / FOCUS
371
Maharashtra & GujaratSeparate development boards for Saurashtra & Kutch (Gujarat) and Vidarbha & Marathwada (Maharashtra)
371-A
NagalandProtection of Naga customary law & religious/social practices; Special powers to Governor
371-B
AssamCommittee of tribal area members in State Legislative Assembly
371-C
ManipurCommittee of hill area members in State Legislative Assembly
371-D
Andhra PradeshEquitable opportunities in public employment & education
371-E
AP (Universities)Establishment of a Central University in Andhra Pradesh
371-F
SikkimSpecial provisions following merger with India (1975)
371-G
MizoramProtection of Mizo customary law & religious/social practices
371-H
Arunachal PradeshSpecial responsibility of Governor regarding law & order
371-I
GoaMembership of State Legislature
371-J
KarnatakaSpecial provisions for Hyderabad-Karnataka region
🪪 PART D : CITIZENSHIP — Articles 5 to 11 Arts. 5–11
ARTICLESUBJECT MATTER
Art. 5
Citizenship at commencement (26 Jan 1950) — Born in India / Parent born in India / Resident 5 years before CoI
Art. 6
Persons migrated from Pakistan — Direct citizenship if before 19th July 1948; Registration (6 months residence) if after
Art. 7
Persons migrated to Pakistan on/after 1st March 1947 but later returned to India
Art. 8
Rights of citizenship of persons of Indian origin residing outside India
Art. 9
NO dual citizenship — voluntarily acquiring foreign citizenship = loses Indian citizenship
Art. 10
Continuance of the rights of citizenship
Art. 11
Parliament has power to regulate citizenship by law → Citizenship Act, 1955
📋 PART E : CITIZENSHIP ACT 1955 — Acquisition & Loss 5 Ways + 3 Ways
✅ ACQUISITION OF CITIZENSHIP — 5 Ways
1
Birth (Law of Soil)
Born in India
2
Descent (Law of Blood)
Born outside India — one parent is Indian citizen
3
Registration
2–3 generations abroad with Indian-origin parents/grandparents → 7 years residence required
4
Naturalization (non-Indian)
12 years residence + knowledge of one Indian language (8th Schedule)
5
Incorporation of Territory
When new territory merges with India, its residents may become citizens
❌ LOSS OF CITIZENSHIP — 3 Ways
1
Renunciation
Voluntary relinquishment by citizen's own will (written declaration)
2
Termination
Voluntarily acquiring citizenship of another country (without informing India)
3
Deprivation
Government deprives citizenship due to:
• Fraud / misrepresentation
• Disloyalty to Constitution
• Assisting enemy country
• Imprisonment ≥ 2 years within 5 years of registration/naturalization
📜 PART F : CITIZENSHIP AMENDMENT ACT (CAA) 2019 ⭐ Hot Topic!

🔑 CAA 2019 — Fast-Track Citizenship through Naturalization

Provides fast-track citizenship to persecuted religious minorities from 3 neighbouring countries who entered India on or before 31st December 2014
3 Countries
Pakistan · Bangladesh · Afghanistan
6 Religions Covered
Hindu · Buddhist · Jain · Sikh · Parsi · Christian
Residency Reduced To
6 Years (instead of normal 11 years)
⚠️ Muslims from these 3 countries are NOT covered under CAA 2019
✈️ PART G : INDIAN DIASPORA — OCI, PIO & NRI 3 Categories
🌐 OCI — Overseas Citizen of India (2005)
  • Immigration status (NOT actual citizenship) — live & work in India indefinitely
  • NOT citizens — no voting rights, no political rights
  • Cannot buy agricultural land
  • Pakistanis & Bangladeshis CANNOT apply
  • Not entitled to FR in public employment
  • Established by amending Citizenship Act 1955
🌍 PIO — Person of Indian Origin (2002)
  • Indian ancestry (parents/grandparents Indian) but NOT an Indian citizen
  • Limited rights compared to OCI
  • Cannot hold passport of: Pakistan, Afghanistan, Bangladesh, China, Nepal, Bhutan, Sri Lanka
  • ⭐ KEY In 2015, PIO merged with OCI — PIO holders auto-became OCI holders
🛫 NRI — Non-Resident Indian
  • Holds Indian passport — temporarily outside India
  • Resides outside India for more than 182 days in a financial year
  • CAN vote — full Indian citizenship rights
  • No income tax on foreign income
  • NRI Day: Pravasi Bharatiya Diwas — 9th January
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗India = Union of States (NOT Federation)
States CANNOT secede from Union
✗State Legislature opinion under Art. 3 = NOT binding on Parliament
✗Andhra = 1st state on linguistic basis (1953)
NOT States Reorganisation Act 1956
✗OCI = NOT actual citizenship
NRI = Full Indian citizenship
✗CAA 2019 covers 6 religions — Muslims NOT covered
✗Naturalization = 12 years (normal)
CAA fast-track = 6 years
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Art. 1 = India is a Union of States
★Art. 3 bill = Simple Majority + President's permission
★1st linguistic state = Andhra (1 Oct 1953) — Potti Sriramulu
★Fazl Ali Commission rejected "1 Language – 1 State"
★States Reorg. Act 1956 = 14 States + 6 UTs
★Acquisition = 5 ways | Loss = 3 ways
★PIO merged into OCI in 2015
★NRI Day = 9th January
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st state on linguistic basis → Andhra Pradesh (1953)
➤Andhra agitation led by → Potti Sriramulu
➤Fazl Ali Commission members → Fazl Ali, K.M. Panikkar, H.N. Kunzru
➤OCI established → 2005
➤PIO established → 2002 (merged with OCI in 2015)
➤CAA 2019 — entry deadline → 31st December 2014
➤NRI = outside India > 182 days per financial year
➤Art. 371-F = Sikkim (merged 1975)
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Article 1 declares India to be a what?
Union of States
Q2
Names of States & UTs are listed in which Schedule?
First Schedule
Q3
Which Article deals with formation/alteration of States?
Article 3
Q4
A bill under Article 3 requires whose prior permission?
The President of India
Q5
What majority is needed to pass a bill under Article 3?
Simple Majority
Q6
Is a State Legislature's opinion binding on Parliament under Art. 3?
No — NOT binding
Q7
Which was the first State formed on a linguistic basis?
Andhra Pradesh (1953)
Q8
Who led the agitation for Andhra State?
Potti Sriramulu
Q9
Andhra State was formed on which date?
1st October 1953
Q10
Members of the Fazl Ali Commission?
Fazl Ali, K.M. Panikkar, H.N. Kunzru
Q11
What theory did the Fazl Ali Commission reject?
"One Language — One State"
Q12
States Reorganisation Act 1956 created how many States & UTs?
14 States & 6 Union Territories
Q13
States Reorganisation Act came into effect on?
1st November 1956
Q14
Special provisions for Nagaland are under which Article?
Article 371-A
Q15
Article 371-F provides special provisions for which State?
Sikkim (merged 1975)
Q16
Article 371-J provides special provisions for which State?
Karnataka (Hyderabad-Karnataka)
Q17
Which Article deals with citizenship at commencement?
Article 5
Q18
Which Article says Parliament can regulate citizenship by law?
Article 11
Q19
Which Article prohibits dual citizenship?
Article 9
Q20
How many ways can citizenship be acquired under Citizenship Act 1955?
5 Ways
Q21
How many ways can citizenship be lost under Citizenship Act 1955?
3 Ways
Q22
What is "Law of Soil" in citizenship?
Citizenship by Birth in India
Q23
What is "Law of Blood" in citizenship?
Citizenship by Descent
Q24
How many years of residence required for Naturalization?
12 Years
Q25
What is "Renunciation" of citizenship?
Voluntary relinquishment by citizen
Q26
CAA 2019 covers religious minorities from how many countries?
3 Countries (Pak, Bang, Afghan)
Q27
How many religions are covered under CAA 2019?
6 Religions
Q28
CAA 2019 covers who entered India on or before?
31st December 2014
Q29
Residency period reduced to how many years under CAA 2019?
6 Years (from 11 years)
Q30
Are Muslims from Pakistan covered under CAA 2019?
No — NOT covered
Q31
OCI was established in which year?
2005
Q32
Can an OCI card holder vote in Indian elections?
No — No voting rights
Q33
Can Pakistanis or Bangladeshis apply for OCI?
No — Not eligible
Q34
PIO card scheme was merged with OCI in which year?
2015
Q35
An NRI is a person residing outside India for more than how many days?
182 days per financial year
Q36
Can an NRI vote in Indian elections?
Yes — Can vote
Q37
NRI Day (Pravasi Bharatiya Diwas) is observed on?
9th January
Q38
JVP Committee members were?
Nehru, Patel, Pattabhi Sitaramayya
Q39
Dhar Committee (1948) recommended what regarding linguistic states?
Against linguistic reorganisation
Q40
Enemy aliens do NOT enjoy protection under which Article?
Article 22
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 04 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 04 of 14
Chapter 05 Fundamental Rights — Part III — Arts. 12–35
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Fundamental Rights
Part III — Articles 12 to 35
📖 Chapter 05
🛡️ Magna Carta of India
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ 6 FRs  ●  Arts 12–35  ●  5 Writs  ●  Landmark Cases  ●  Art.32 vs 226  ●  40 MCQs
🛡️ PART A : SIX FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS — Quick Overview Arts. 12–35
📌 KEY FACTS: Originally 7 FRs → Now 6 FRs (Right to Property removed by 44th CAA, 1978 → now Legal Right under Art. 300A)  |  Borrowed from USA's Bill of Rights  |  Part III = "Magna Carta of India"  |  Called "Conscience of the Constitution" by Granville Austin
⚖️ Right to Equality
Art. 14–18
  • Art. 14 — Equality before law & equal protection
  • Art. 15 — No discrimination (religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth)
  • Art. 16 — Equal opportunity in public employment
  • Art. 17 — Abolition of untouchability
  • Art. 18 — Abolition of titles
🗣️ Right to Freedom
Art. 19–22
  • Art. 19 — 6 Freedoms (speech, assembly, association, movement, residence, profession)
  • Art. 20 — Protection in conviction (Ex-post-facto, Double jeopardy, Self-incrimination)
  • Art. 21 — Right to life & personal liberty
  • Art. 21A — Right to elementary education (86th CAA, 2002)
  • Art. 22 — Protection against arrest & detention
⛓️ Right Against Exploitation
Art. 23–24
  • Art. 23 — Prohibition of human trafficking & forced labour
  • Art. 24 — Prohibition of child labour (below 14 yrs in hazardous work)
🕌 Right to Religion
Art. 25–28
  • Art. 25 — Freedom of conscience, profess, practice & propagate
  • Art. 26 — Freedom to manage religious affairs
  • Art. 27 — Freedom from tax for promotion of religion
  • Art. 28 — Freedom from religious instruction in State schools
🏫 Cultural & Educational Rights
Art. 29–30
  • Art. 29 — Protection of minority interests (language, script, culture)
  • Art. 30 — Minorities' right to establish & administer educational institutions
⚖️ Right to Constitutional Remedies
Art. 32–35
  • Art. 32 — Move Supreme Court (part of Basic Structure)
  • Art. 33 — FRs of armed forces (Parliament can restrict)
  • Art. 34 — Martial law restrictions
  • Art. 35 — Parliament's exclusive power to legislate on FRs
📋 PART B : KEY FEATURES + Articles 12 & 13 Foundation

⚡ Key Features of Fundamental Rights

  • NOT Absolute — Qualified (subject to reasonable restrictions)
  • Justiciable — Can be enforced through courts
  • Not Permanent — Can be amended (but not Basic Structure)
  • Arts. 20 & 21 CANNOT be suspended even during National Emergency
  • Art. 19 suspended only during External Emergency (NOT internal)
  • Citizen-specific: Arts. 15, 16, 19, 29, 30 only for citizens
  • FRs vs DPSP → FRs always prevail
  • Right to Property → Legal Right (Art. 300A) — removed by 44th CAA 1978

📜 Article 12 — Definition of 'State' & Article 13 — Judicial Review

  • Art. 12 'State' includes: Govt & Parliament of India, State Govts & Legislatures, All local authorities (Municipalities, Panchayats), All other authorities (LIC, ONGC, SAIL)
  • Art. 13: Any law inconsistent with FRs shall be VOID
  • Provides for Judicial Review
  • Constitutional Amendment is NOT a 'law' under Art. 13
  • ⭐ KEY Kesavananda Bharati (1973) — even CAA can be challenged if it violates Basic Structure
⚖️ PART C : RIGHT TO EQUALITY — Articles 14 to 18 Arts. 14–18

Art. 14 — Equality Before Law vs Equal Protection

  • Equality Before Law (British — Negative) → No person above law; A.V. Dicey's Rule of Law
  • Equal Protection of Laws (American — Positive) → Like should be treated alike
  • Available to ALL persons — citizens, foreigners & legal persons
  • Exceptions: President, Governor, Foreign sovereigns, diplomats, UNO

Art. 15 — No Discrimination + Art. 16 — Equal Employment

  • Art. 15 — No discrimination on: religion, race, caste, sex, place of birth
  • Art. 15(3) — Special provisions for women & children
  • Art. 15(5) — OBC, SC, ST reservation in educational institutions (93rd CAA)
  • Art. 15(6) — EWS reservation (103rd CAA, 2019)
  • Art. 16 — Equal opportunity in public employment for citizens only
  • ⭐ KEY Mandal Commission (1979) → 27% OBC reservation
  • Indra Sawhney Case (1992) → 50% cap on total reservation; creamy layer concept
  • Art. 16(6) → EWS reservation up to 10%

Art. 17 — Abolition of Untouchability

  • Forbids untouchability in ANY form
  • Untouchability (Offences) Act 1955 → renamed Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1976
  • 'Untouchability' is NOT defined in Constitution or Act
  • Available against private individuals also (not just State)

Art. 18 — Abolition of Titles

  • State shall NOT confer any title except military or academic distinctions
  • 1996 SC Ruling — Bharat Ratna, Padma awards are constitutionally valid — NOT 'titles' under Art. 18
  • No foreign title without President's consent
🗣️ PART D : RIGHT TO FREEDOM — Articles 19 to 22 Arts. 19–22
Article 19 — Six Fundamental Freedoms (Citizens Only)
19(1)(a) Speech & Expression
Includes press freedom, right to internet (2020), right to silence. NOT: right to strike
19(1)(b) Assembly
Peaceful, unarmed only. 5+ persons = unlawful assembly under Sec 141 IPC. No right to strike
19(1)(c) Association
Form unions. Cooperative societies added by 97th CAA, 2011. Includes right NOT to join
19(1)(d) Movement
Move freely throughout India. Promotes national unity
19(1)(e) Residence
Reside & settle anywhere in India. Tribal areas exempt
19(1)(g) Profession/Trade
All means of livelihood. State can prescribe qualifications or create monopoly

Art. 20 — 3 Protections Against Conviction

  • (a) No Ex-Post-Facto Law — Cannot penalise for acts legal when done. Only criminal law, NOT civil/tax
  • (b) No Double Jeopardy — No prosecution twice for same offence
  • (c) No Self-Incrimination — Cannot be compelled to be witness against oneself. Oral & documentary both

Art. 21 — Right to Life + Art. 21A — Education

  • ⭐ KEY Golden Trinity — Arts. 14 + 19 + 21 together
  • Gopalan Case (1950) → Narrow: protects from executive action only
  • Maneka Gandhi (1978) → Wide: procedure must be reasonable, fair & just
  • Puttaswamy Case (2017) → Right to Privacy under Art. 21
  • Olga Tellis (1985) → Right to livelihood part of Art. 21
  • Art. 21A (86th CAA, 2002) — Free & compulsory education for children aged 6–14 years
  • RTE Act passed 2009, enforced 2010
Art. 22 — Protection Against Arrest & Detention
22(1) — Ordinary Law Arrest
  • Right to be informed of grounds of arrest
  • Right to consult & be defended by a lawyer
  • Produced before magistrate within 24 hours
  • Released after 24 hours unless magistrate authorises
  • NOT available to enemy aliens or preventive detention
22(2) — Preventive Detention
  • Available to both citizens AND aliens
  • Detention max 3 months without Advisory Board approval
  • Advisory Board = judges of a High Court
  • Parliament has exclusive authority for defence, foreign affairs & security laws
🔒 PART E : EXPLOITATION, RELIGION & CULTURAL RIGHTS — Arts. 23–30 Arts. 23–30

Art. 23 & 24 — Right Against Exploitation

  • Art. 23 — Prohibits human trafficking & forced/bonded labour
  • Available to both citizens AND non-citizens
  • State CAN impose compulsory service for public purposes (military, wartime)
  • Art. 24 — Children below 14 years prohibited in factories, mines, hazardous work
  • Can work in safe places during non-school hours

Arts. 25–28 — Right to Freedom of Religion

  • Art. 25 — Freedom to profess, practice & propagate religion
  • Art. 26 — Religious denomination can establish, manage & own property
  • Art. 27 — No compulsion to pay taxes for promotion of religion
  • Art. 28 — No religious instruction in wholly State-funded institutions
  • India follows Positive Secularism — State IS involved in religious matters

Arts. 29 & 30 — Cultural & Educational Rights

  • Art. 29 — Any section with distinct language, script or culture can conserve it
  • No denial of admission on grounds of religion, race, caste, language
  • Constitution does NOT define 'minorities'
  • Art. 29 applies to ALL sections (majority too), Art. 30 = minorities ONLY
  • Art. 30 — Minorities right to establish & administer educational institutions
  • State shall NOT discriminate while granting aid to minority institutions

Arts. 32–35 — Right to Constitutional Remedies

  • ⭐ KEY Art. 32 = "Heart & Soul of Constitution" (Dr. Ambedkar)
  • Art. 32 is part of Basic Structure — cannot be suspended except by Constitution
  • SC CANNOT refuse to exercise writ jurisdiction — mandatory duty
  • Art. 33 — Parliament can restrict FRs of armed forces, police, intelligence
  • Art. 34 — Martial law restrictions (borrowed from English common law)
  • Art. 35 — Parliament's exclusive power to legislate on certain FRs
📜 PART F : FIVE WRITS — Types & Scope ⭐ Most Asked!
WRITMEANINGISSUED AGAINSTKEY EXCEPTION / NOTE
Habeas Corpus
"To have the body"
 Produce detained person before court Both public & private authorities NOT issued when: detention is lawful / by competent court / outside jurisdiction
Mandamus
"We command"
 Commands public official to perform official duty Public body, corporation, inferior court, tribunal, govt CANNOT be issued against: private individuals / discretionary duty / President / Governor / CJ of HC (judicial)
Prohibition
"To forbid"
 Higher court PREVENTS lower court from exceeding jurisdiction — directs INACTIVITY Judicial & quasi-judicial authorities only NOT against: administrative / legislative bodies / private individuals
Certiorari
"To be certified"
 Transfers or squashes order of lower court — both PREVENTIVE & CURATIVE Judicial & quasi-judicial. 1991: also against administrative authority NOT against: legislative bodies / private individuals
Quo Warranto
"By what authority"
 Enquires into legality of claim to a public office Any holder of substantive public office CANNOT be issued for: ministerial or private office. Can be sought by ANY interested person
Art. 32 — Supreme Court vs Art. 226 — High Court
Jurisdiction
Art. 32 = Original but NOT exclusive | Art. 226 = Exclusive & original
Scope
Art. 32 = Only FRs | Art. 226 = FRs + legal + constitutional + customary rights
Can refuse?
Art. 32 = SC CANNOT refuse (mandatory) | Art. 226 = HC MAY refuse (discretionary)
Territory
Art. 32 = WIDER (all India) | Art. 226 = Narrower
⚖️ Landmark Cases — Quick Reference
CASEKEY RULING
Gopalan (1950)Art. 21 — narrow; only executive action
Maneka Gandhi (1978)Art. 21 — wide; fair, just & reasonable
Kesavananda (1973)Basic Structure; CAA challengeable
Indra Sawhney (1992)27% OBC; 50% cap; creamy layer
Olga Tellis (1985)Right to livelihood = Art. 21
Puttaswamy (2017)Right to Privacy = Art. 21
Mohini JainRight to education flows from Art. 21
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗Originally 7 FRs → Now 6 FRs
Right to Property removed by 44th CAA 1978
✗Arts. 20 & 21 CANNOT be suspended even during National Emergency
✗Art. 19 suspended only during External Emergency, NOT internal
✗Art. 29 = ALL sections | Art. 30 = Minorities ONLY
✗Prohibition = INACTIVITY (prevent)
Certiorari = BOTH preventive & curative
✗Mandamus CANNOT be issued against President, Governor or discretionary duty
✅ RAPID RECALL
★FRs = "Magna Carta of India" | "Conscience of Constitution" (Austin)
★Art. 32 = "Heart & Soul of Constitution" (Ambedkar)
★Golden Trinity = Arts. 14 + 19 + 21
★Mandal Commission (1979) → 27% OBC reservation
★Art. 21A (86th CAA, 2002) → Education 6–14 yrs
★Preventive detention max = 3 months without Advisory Board
★Art. 17 — Untouchability available against private individuals also
★Art. 19(1)(c) — cooperative societies added by 97th CAA 2011
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤FRs borrowed from → USA's Bill of Rights
➤1st Backward Class Commission → Kelkar Commission (1953)
➤2nd BC Commission → Mandal Commission (1979)
➤EWS reservation added by → 103rd CAA, 2019
➤Protection of Civil Rights Act → 1976 (originally 1955)
➤RTE Act passed 2009, enforced 2010
➤Right to Internet = Art. 19(1)(a) → 2020 (Kashmir case)
➤Martial law borrowed from → English common law
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Fundamental Rights are enshrined in which Part of the Constitution?
Part III (Arts. 12–35)
Q2
Part III is described as the what of India?
Magna Carta of India
Q3
Who called FRs the "Conscience of the Constitution"?
Granville Austin
Q4
Fundamental Rights concept was borrowed from?
USA's Bill of Rights
Q5
Originally how many FRs? Now how many?
Originally 7, Now 6
Q6
Right to Property was removed by which amendment?
44th CAA, 1978
Q7
Which FRs are available only to citizens (not foreigners)?
Arts. 15, 16, 19, 29, 30
Q8
Which two FRs CANNOT be suspended even during National Emergency?
Articles 20 & 21
Q9
Article 14 — "Equality Before Law" concept is borrowed from?
UK (A.V. Dicey's Rule of Law)
Q10
"Equal Protection of Laws" (Art. 14) is borrowed from?
USA Constitution
Q11
Article 15 prohibits discrimination on which grounds?
Religion, Race, Caste, Sex, Place of Birth
Q12
Which amendment added EWS reservation under Art. 15(6)?
103rd CAA, 2019
Q13
Mandal Commission recommended what % reservation for OBC?
27% OBC Reservation
Q14
Indra Sawhney Case (1992) imposed what cap on total reservations?
50% Cap
Q15
Protection of Civil Rights Act was enacted in which year?
1976 (originally 1955)
Q16
'Untouchability' is defined where in the Constitution?
NOT defined anywhere
Q17
Bharat Ratna and Padma awards are valid under which SC ruling?
1996 SC Ruling (NOT titles under Art. 18)
Q18
How many Freedoms are under Article 19?
6 Freedoms
Q19
Right to Internet falls under which Article?
Art. 19(1)(a) — 2020
Q20
Cooperative societies were added to Art. 19(1)(c) by which CAA?
97th CAA, 2011
Q21
What are the three protections under Article 20?
No Ex-Post-Facto, No Double Jeopardy, No Self-Incrimination
Q22
Which case gave a narrow interpretation of Art. 21?
Gopalan Case, 1950
Q23
Which case gave a wide interpretation of Art. 21?
Maneka Gandhi Case, 1978
Q24
Right to Privacy is a FR under which Article? (which case)
Art. 21 — Puttaswamy Case 2017
Q25
Arts. 14, 19 & 21 together are called?
Golden Trinity of FRs
Q26
Art. 21A was added by which amendment and gives education to whom?
86th CAA 2002 — Children 6–14 yrs
Q27
Preventive detention cannot exceed how many months without Advisory Board?
3 Months
Q28
Child labour prohibited below how many years under Art. 24?
14 Years
Q29
Which article provides freedom to profess, practice & propagate religion?
Article 25
Q30
Article 30 rights are available to whom?
Minorities ONLY (religious & linguistic)
Q31
Art. 32 is called the what by Dr. Ambedkar?
"Heart & Soul of the Constitution"
Q32
Which writ means "To have the body"?
Habeas Corpus
Q33
Which writ commands a public official to perform his duty?
Mandamus ("We Command")
Q34
Which writ prevents a lower court from exceeding jurisdiction?
Prohibition ("To Forbid")
Q35
Which writ is both preventive AND curative?
Certiorari
Q36
Which writ enquires into legality of claim to a public office?
Quo Warranto
Q37
HC can enforce rights under Art. 226 but SC under Art. 32 — key difference?
Art. 226 = FRs + legal rights | Art. 32 = Only FRs
Q38
Martial law concept in Art. 34 was borrowed from?
English Common Law
Q39
Art. 35 gives exclusive power to make FR-related laws to whom?
Parliament only (not State Legislatures)
Q40
First Backward Classes Commission (1953) was headed by?
Kelkar Commission (1953)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 05 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 05 of 14
Chapter 06 DPSP & Fundamental Duties — Part IV + IVA
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
DPSP & Fundamental Duties
Part IV (Arts. 36–51) & Part IVA (Art. 51A)
📖 Chapter 06
🇮🇪 Borrowed from Ireland
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ DPSP Classification  ●  All Articles 36–51  ●  FR vs DPSP  ●  11 Fundamental Duties  ●  Landmark Cases  ●  40 MCQs
📜 PART A : DIRECTIVE PRINCIPLES OF STATE POLICY — Overview Arts. 36–51
📌 Constitutional Framework
  • Located in Part IV — Articles 36 to 51
  • Borrowed from Irish Constitution of 1937 (which drew from Spanish Constitution)
  • Inspired by Karachi Resolution (1931) of INC & GoI Act 1935
  • Non-Justiciable — cannot be enforced by courts
  • Fundamental for governance — courts cannot invalidate laws violating DPSPs
  • Positive obligations on the State (unlike FRs which are negative)
  • Represent Social & Economic Democracy
🎓 Famous Descriptions
  • Dr. B.R. Ambedkar — called DPSPs the "Novel Features" of the Constitution
  • Granville Austin — FRs + DPSPs together = "Conscience of the Constitution"
  • K.T. Shah — criticised as "a bank cheque payable at the Bank's convenience"
  • B.N. Rau — described as "Moral precepts for authority of State"
📂 PART B : THREE CATEGORIES OF DPSPs 3 Types
🏛️ SOCIALISTIC
Focus: Socio-economic justice, welfare state, democratic socialist ideology
Reduce inequality, protect workers, ensure livelihood
Arts: 38, 39, 39A, 41, 42, 43, 43A, 47
🌾 GANDHIAN
Focus: Gandhi's reconstruction programme, rural upliftment, village-centric governance
Panchayati Raj, cottage industries, prohibition
Arts: 40, 43, 43B, 46, 47, 48
💡 LIBERAL-INTELLECTUAL
Focus: Individual rights, liberties, international relations, intellectual freedom
Uniform Civil Code, environment, international peace
Arts: 44, 45, 48A, 49, 50, 51
📋 PART C : ARTICLE-WISE DPSP BREAKDOWN — Arts. 36 to 51 All Articles
ARTICLESUBJECT / DIRECTIVECATEGORY
Art. 36
Definition of 'State' for Part IV (same as Art. 12 for FRs)Foundational
Art. 37
Application of principles — Non-justiciable but fundamental for governanceFoundational
Art. 38
Welfare State — justice: social, economic & political; minimise inequalities [44th CAA added 2nd clause]Socialistic
Art. 39
(a) Livelihood (b) Distribution of resources (c) No concentration of wealth (d) Equal pay for equal work (e) Health of workers (f) Healthy development of children [42nd CAA]Socialistic
Art. 39A
Free Legal Aid & Equal Justice to the poor [42nd CAA, 1976]Socialistic
Art. 40
Panchayati Raj — Organisation of Village PanchayatsGandhian
Art. 41
Right to work, education & public assistance (unemployment, old age, sickness)Socialistic
Art. 42
Just & Humane Conditions of Work; Maternity leaveSocialistic
Art. 43
Living Wages for workers; Cottage industriesSocialistic / Gandhian
Art. 43A
Participation of workers in management of industries [42nd CAA, 1976]Socialistic
Art. 43B
Promotion of Co-operative Societies — voluntary, autonomous, democratic [97th CAA, 2011]Gandhian
Art. 44
Uniform Civil Code (UCC) across IndiaLiberal
Art. 45
Early Childhood Care for children 0–6 years [amended by 86th CAA, 2002]Gandhian
Art. 46
Promotion of educational & economic interests of SC, ST & weaker sectionsGandhian
Art. 47
Raise nutrition & standard of living; Prohibit intoxicating drinks and drugsGandhian
Art. 48
Agriculture & Animal Husbandry; prohibit slaughter of cows, calves, milch/draught cattleGandhian
Art. 48A
Environment protection & safeguarding forests & wildlife [42nd CAA, 1976]Liberal
Art. 49
Protection of monuments, places & objects of national/historical importanceLiberal
Art. 50
Separation of Judiciary from Executive in public servicesLiberal
Art. 51
International peace & security — promote peace, respect international law, settlement by arbitrationLiberal
⚖️ PART D : FRs vs DPSPs + LANDMARK CASES ⭐ Very Important!
FUNDAMENTAL RIGHTS (FRs)DIRECTIVE PRINCIPLES (DPSPs)
Negative obligations on the StatePositive obligations on the State
Justiciable — legally enforceableNon-justiciable — not enforceable
Represent Political DemocracyRepresent Social & Economic Democracy
Automatically enforced by courtsRequire legislative enactment to implement
Courts can invalidate laws violating FRsCourts CANNOT invalidate laws violating DPSP
Individual-centric — protect citizensState-directed — guide policy formation
1951
Champakam Dorairajan Case
  • ⭐ KEY First major case on FR vs DPSP conflict
  • SC held: FRs prevail over DPSPs; FRs are amendable
  • Violation of Art. 15(1) and Art. 29(2) found
1967
Golaknath Case
  • SC held: Parliament CANNOT take away FRs
  • Parliament's response: 24th CAA — Parliament CAN amend FRs
  • 25th CAA — Laws implementing Art. 39(b) & (c) not void even if violating Arts. 14 or 19
1973
Kesavananda Bharati Case
  • ⭐ KEY Decided by 13-judge bench — largest Constitutional bench in Indian history
  • Parliament CAN amend FRs but CANNOT disturb Basic Structure
  • 25th CAA: 1st provision upheld; 2nd provision (barring judicial review) declared unconstitutional
1980
Minerva Mills Case
  • Constitution founded on balance between FRs and DPSPs
  • Neither FRs nor DPSPs can be given absolute primacy over the other
  • Reaffirmed Basic Structure doctrine
⚡ Constitutional Amendments Adding New DPSPs
AMENDMENTARTICLEADDITION / CHANGE
42nd CAA
1976		Art. 39(f)State to provide opportunities for healthy development of children
Art. 39AEqual justice and free legal aid to the poor
Art. 43AWorkers' participation in management of industries
Art. 48AProtection and improvement of environment; safeguarding forests & wildlife
44th CAA
1978		Art. 38Minimise inequalities in income, status, facilities and opportunities
86th CAA
2002		Art. 45Changed from elementary education to early childhood care (0–6 yrs); Art. 21A made education a FR
97th CAA
2011		Art. 43BPromotion of co-operative societies — voluntary, autonomous, democratic
🤝 PART E : FUNDAMENTAL DUTIES — Article 51A (Part IVA) 11 Duties
📌 Key Facts about Fundamental Duties
  • Located in Part IVA — Only Article 51A
  • Added by 42nd CAA, 1976 — Based on Sardar Swaran Singh Committee recommendations
  • Committee recommended 8 duties; Parliament enacted 10 duties
  • Inspired by Constitution of former USSR
  • Applicable only to Indian citizens (NOT aliens/foreigners)
  • Non-justiciable in nature
  • 86th CAA, 2002 added 11th duty (clause k)
  • Total: ⭐ 11 Duties — clauses (a) to (k)
⚡ TRICK to Remember All 11 Duties
C N S N B C E S P E E
Constitution | National Struggle | Sovereignty | National Defence | Brotherhood
Cultural Heritage | Environment | Scientific Temper | Public Property | Excellence | Education (k)
(a)
Constitutional Fidelity
Abide by the Constitution; respect its ideals, institutions, National Flag & National Anthem
(b)
National Struggle
Cherish & follow noble ideals that inspired the national struggle for freedom
(c)
Sovereignty & Integrity
Uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India
(d)
National Defence
Defend the country and render national service when called upon
(e)
Brotherhood & Dignity
Promote harmony & spirit of common brotherhood; renounce practices derogatory to dignity of women
(f)
Cultural Heritage
Value and preserve the rich heritage of India's composite culture
(g)
Environment
Protect and improve natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers & wildlife; compassion for living creatures
(h)
Scientific Temper
Develop scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform
(i)
Public Property
Safeguard public property and abjure violence
(j)
Excellence
Strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity
(k)
Education ⭐ 86th CAA 2002
Provide opportunities for education to child/ward between ages of 6 and 14 years
💡 Quick Count
Swaran Singh Committee recommended: 8 duties
Parliament enacted (42nd CAA): 10 duties
86th CAA (2002) added clause (k): Total = 11 duties
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗DPSPs borrowed from Ireland, NOT USSR
Fundamental Duties borrowed from USSR
✗DPSPs = Non-justiciable
FRs = Justiciable
✗Art. 44 = UCC  |  Art. 45 = Child care (0–6 yrs)
Art. 50 = Separation of Judiciary
✗Swaran Singh Committee → 8 duties recommended
Parliament enacted → 10 duties
✗11th duty (k) added by 86th CAA 2002
NOT by 42nd CAA 1976
✗FRs conflict → Champakam (1951) = First case
Not Golaknath or Kesavananda
✅ RAPID RECALL
★DPSP = Part IV | Arts. 36–51 | Ireland
★Ambedkar = "Novel Features" | Austin = "Conscience of Constitution"
★Art. 39(d) = Equal pay for equal work
★Art. 39A = Free legal aid (42nd CAA)
★Art. 44 = UCC | Art. 48 = Cow slaughter prohibited
★Fundamental Duties = Part IVA | Art. 51A | 11 duties
★FDs = citizens only | Non-justiciable
★Minerva Mills 1980 = Balance between FRs & DPSPs
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤DPSPs source → Irish Constitution 1937
➤Inspired by → Karachi Resolution 1931 + GoI Act 1935
➤1st FR vs DPSP case → Champakam Dorairajan 1951
➤Largest Constitutional bench → 13 judges (Kesavananda 1973)
➤FDs added by → 42nd CAA 1976
➤FDs based on → Sardar Swaran Singh Committee
➤FDs inspired by → USSR Constitution
➤Art. 43B (Co-op societies) → 97th CAA 2011
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
DPSPs are located in which Part and Articles of the Constitution?
Part IV — Arts. 36 to 51
Q2
DPSPs were borrowed from which country's constitution?
Ireland (Irish Constitution, 1937)
Q3
Who described DPSPs as "Novel Features" of the Constitution?
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar
Q4
Who called DPSPs "a bank cheque payable at the Bank's convenience"?
K.T. Shah
Q5
Who described DPSPs as "Moral precepts for authority of State"?
B.N. Rau
Q6
Are DPSPs justiciable (enforceable in courts)?
No — Non-justiciable
Q7
Which Article directs Equal Pay for Equal Work?
Article 39(d)
Q8
Which Article directs Free Legal Aid to the poor?
Article 39A (42nd CAA, 1976)
Q9
Which Article directs Organisation of Village Panchayats?
Article 40 (Panchayati Raj)
Q10
Which Article directs Living Wages for workers?
Article 43
Q11
Which Article directs Uniform Civil Code across India?
Article 44
Q12
Which Article directs Early Childhood Care (0–6 years)?
Article 45 (amended by 86th CAA 2002)
Q13
Which Article directs Separation of Judiciary from Executive?
Article 50
Q14
Which Article deals with International Peace & Security?
Article 51
Q15
Which Article deals with Environment Protection? (added by which CAA?)
Article 48A — 42nd CAA, 1976
Q16
Which Article directs promotion of Co-operative Societies?
Article 43B (97th CAA, 2011)
Q17
Which Article deals with Workers' Participation in Management?
Article 43A (42nd CAA, 1976)
Q18
DPSPs represent which type of democracy?
Social & Economic Democracy
Q19
FRs represent which type of democracy?
Political Democracy
Q20
Which was the FIRST major case on FR vs DPSP conflict?
Champakam Dorairajan Case, 1951
Q21
Which case held that Parliament CANNOT take away FRs?
Golaknath Case, 1967
Q22
Which amendment was Parliament's response to the Golaknath Case?
24th CAA (Parliament CAN amend FRs)
Q23
Kesavananda Bharati Case was decided by how many judges?
13-judge bench (largest ever)
Q24
What did Minerva Mills Case 1980 establish?
Balance between FRs & DPSPs — neither has absolute primacy
Q25
Which Gandhian DPSP prohibits slaughter of cows and cattle?
Article 48
Q26
Fundamental Duties are located in which Part and Article?
Part IVA — Article 51A
Q27
Fundamental Duties were added by which Constitutional Amendment?
42nd CAA, 1976
Q28
FDs were based on recommendations of which committee?
Sardar Swaran Singh Committee (1976)
Q29
FDs were inspired by which country's constitution?
USSR (Former Soviet Union)
Q30
How many Fundamental Duties did the Swaran Singh Committee recommend?
8 Duties
Q31
How many FDs did Parliament enact through 42nd CAA?
10 Duties
Q32
What is the total number of Fundamental Duties currently?
11 Duties (a to k)
Q33
Which amendment added the 11th Fundamental Duty (clause k)?
86th CAA, 2002
Q34
What does the 11th Fundamental Duty (k) state?
Provide education to child/ward (6–14 years)
Q35
Are Fundamental Duties applicable to foreigners?
No — Only Indian Citizens
Q36
Are Fundamental Duties justiciable in nature?
No — Non-justiciable
Q37
Which DPSP was inspired by Karachi Resolution 1931 of INC?
DPSPs as a whole (their origin)
Q38
Art. 38 (Welfare State) had its 2nd clause added by which CAA?
44th CAA, 1978
Q39
Duty to protect environment and wildlife is under which clause of Art. 51A?
Clause (g)
Q40
Duty to develop scientific temper is under which clause of Art. 51A?
Clause (h)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 06 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 06 of 14
Chapter 07 The Union — Parliament — Arts. 52–151
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
The Union — President, VP, PM
Parliament & Legislative Procedures
📖 Chapter 07
🏛️ Part V — Arts. 52–151
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ President  ●  VP  ●  PM  ●  Parliament  ●  Bills  ●  Writs  ●  Parliamentary Terms  ●  40 MCQs
🏛️ PART A : PRESIDENT OF INDIA — Arts. 52–72 1st Citizen
🗳️ Election (Art. 54–55)
  • Elected Indirectly — NOT directly by people
  • Electoral College: Elected MPs (LS+RS) + Elected MLAs of all States + Elected MLAs of Delhi & Puducherry
  • Nominated MPs do NOT participate
  • Method: Proportional Representation via STV + Secret Ballot
  • Proposers: 50 + Seconders: 50 | Security deposit: ₹15,000 in RBI
  • Disputes decided by Supreme Court (final — Art. 71)
📋 Qualifications & Term
  • Indian citizen; age ≥ 35 years
  • Qualified for election as Lok Sabha member
  • Must NOT hold office of profit
  • Oath by CJI (in absence → senior-most SC judge)
  • Term: 5 years — Re-election: Any number of times
  • Resignation: Written letter to Vice President
  • Vacancy filled within 6 months
⚖️ Impeachment (Art. 61)
  • Ground: Violation of Constitution
  • Quasi-judicial in nature
  • Initiated by either House (involves elected + nominated MPs)
  • 14 days prior notice — signed by ¼ of total members
  • 1st House passes with ⅔ majority of total membership
  • 2nd House investigates → passes with ⅔ majority → President removed
⚖️ Pardoning Powers of President (Art. 72) — 5 Types
PARDON
Removes BOTH punishment AND judgement — complete acquittal
COMMUTATION
Replaces penalty with less rigorous one (type changes)
REMISSION
Reduces duration/quantum without changing nature
RESPITE
Reduced punishment for special reason (pregnancy, disability)
REPRIEVE
Temporary stay of death penalty pending further action
⚠️ Key: President (Art. 72) CAN pardon death sentence & court-martial cases | Governor (Art. 161) CANNOT pardon these two
TYPE OF EMERGENCYARTICLEGROUNDS
National EmergencyArt. 352War, External Aggression, Armed Rebellion
President's Rule (State Emergency)Art. 356 & 365Failure of Constitutional machinery in State
Financial EmergencyArt. 360Threat to financial stability/credit of India
🚫 Veto Powers of President (Art. 111)
ABSOLUTE VETO
Total rejection — private member bills / bills where cabinet has resigned. India ✔
SUSPENSIVE VETO
Return for reconsideration — Parliament can override with ordinary majority. India ✔
POCKET VETO
No action indefinitely — stronger than USA. No time limit. India ✔
QUALIFIED VETO
Override requires special majority in legislature. Only USA ✔ (NOT India)
👤 PART B : VICE PRESIDENT OF INDIA — Arts. 63–71 2nd Citizen
🗳️ Election & Qualifications
  • Elected by ALL MPs (elected + nominated) of BOTH Houses — NO MLAs
  • Method: Proportional Representation via STV + Secret Ballot
  • Oath administered by: President or person appointed by him
  • Disputes: Only Supreme Court (binding decision)
  • Election held within 60 days (Art. 68)
  • Qualification for Rajya Sabha (NOT Lok Sabha — key diff from President)
  • Citizen of India, age ≥ 35 years, not hold office of profit
⚙️ Term, Removal & Powers
  • Term: 5 years | Resignation: Written letter to President
  • Removal: Effective majority of RS members (initiated ONLY in RS) + simple majority of LS
  • 14 days prior notice required
  • Ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha (Art. 64)
  • Acts as President during vacancy — maximum 6 months (Art. 65)
  • Modelled on American Vice President
  • Chairman CANNOT certify Money Bill or preside over Joint Sitting
FEATUREPRESIDENT'S ELECTORAL COLLEGEVP'S ELECTORAL COLLEGE
MembersElected MPs + Elected MLAs (all States + Delhi, Puducherry)ALL MPs (Elected + Nominated) — No MLAs
Nominated MPsDo NOT participateDO participate
State LegislaturesParticipate (all states + Delhi, Puducherry)Do NOT participate
🏛️ PART C : PRIME MINISTER & COUNCIL OF MINISTERS — Arts. 74–78 3rd Citizen
🎯 Key Constitutional Provisions
  • ⭐ KEY President = De jure (nominal) executive | PM = De facto (real) executive
  • Art. 74: CoM with PM at head to aid and advise the President
  • 44th CAA 1978: President may require CoM to reconsider advice; must act on reconsidered advice
  • Art. 75(1A) — 91st CAA 2003: Total Ministers (incl. PM) ≤ 15% of LS strength
  • Art. 75(2): Ministers collectively responsible to Lok Sabha
  • PM need not be LS member at appointment — must acquire within 6 months
  • PMs from Rajya Sabha: Indira Gandhi (briefly), H.D. Deve Gowda, Manmohan Singh
🏢 Bodies Chaired by PM
BODYYEAR
NITI Aayog (replaced Planning Commission)2015
National Development Council (NDC)1952
National Water Resources Council1983
Inter-State Council (under Art. 263)1990
National Integration Council1961
National Ganga Council2016
⚖️ PART D : ATTORNEY GENERAL OF INDIA — Art. 76 Highest Law Officer
Appointment & QualificationAppointed by President; qualification = judge of SC. Holds office during pleasure of President
Duties & RightsAdvise GoI on legal matters; right of audience in all courts in India
LimitationsNOT a full-time counsel; NOT a govt servant. Can practice privately but NOT against GoI
First AG: M.C. Setalvad (longest — 1950–63)  |  Solicitor General: Secondary law officer (assists AG — NOT a constitutional body)  |  Advocate General (Art. 165): State's highest law officer, appointed by Governor
🏛️ PART E : PARLIAMENT OF INDIA — Arts. 79–122 LS + RS + President
🔵 RAJYA SABHA — Council of States
250
Maximum strength (238 elected + 12 nominated by President)
245
Current strength (233 from States + 12 nominated)
6 yrs
Member term | ⅓ retire every 2 years
∞
Permanent body — NOT dissolved
30 yrs
Minimum age for members
  • Symbol: Red Carpet → Royalty → Lotus flower
  • First constituted 1952; first sitting: 13 May 1952
  • ⭐ Special Powers (Art. 249, 312) — Can legislate on State subjects (⅔ majority); create new All-India Services (⅔ majority)
🟢 LOK SABHA — House of the People
550
Maximum strength (530 States + 20 UTs)
543
Present strength (524 States + 19 UTs)
5 yrs
Duration from 1st sitting — auto dissolves
25 yrs
Minimum age for members
FPTP
First-Past-The-Post system — single member constituencies
  • Symbol: Green Carpet → Agriculture → Peacock
  • 104th CAA 2019 removed Anglo-Indian nominated seats (2 seats)
  • Can be extended by 1 year at a time during National Emergency
POSTHOW CHOSENFIRST HOLDERKEY POWER / NOTE
Speaker (LS)Elected by LS membersG.V. MavalankarCertifies Money Bill; presides over Joint Sitting; casting vote only
Deputy Speaker (LS)Elected by LS membersM. Ananthasayanam AyyangarActs when Speaker absent; 1st Tribal Speaker: P.A. Sangma; 1st Female: Meira Kumar
Chairman (RS)VP of India (ex-officio)Dr. S. RadhakrishnanCANNOT certify Money Bill or preside over Joint Sitting
Deputy Chairman (RS)Elected by RS membersS.V. Krishnamoorthy Rao1st Female Dy. Chairman: Smt. Violet Alva
📚 PART F : PARLIAMENTARY PROCEDURES & KEY TERMS Must Know!
TERMDEFINITION & KEY POINTS
Question Hour
11AM–12PM. Starred (oral, supplementary allowed) | Unstarred (written, 10-day notice, no supplementary) | Short Notice (urgent, Speaker decides)
Zero Hour
Starts at 12PM (noon). Members raise various issues — Indian Parliamentary invention
Adjournment Motion
Urgent public importance; needs 50+ members' support; Speaker grants permission; notice before sitting commences
Cut Motion
Seeks reduction in a demand. Types: Policy Cut (reduce by ₹1) | Economy Cut (specific amount) | Token Cut (reduce by ₹100, specific grievance)
No-Confidence Motion
Against full CoM; if passed, government must resign. Introduced in LS only
Censure Motion
Against CoM or individual Minister; must specify reasons/charges; moved in LS
Quorum (Art. 100)
Minimum = 1/10 of total House membership to transact business
Guillotine
On last allotted day, Speaker puts all outstanding demands for grants to vote — concludes discussion
Vote on Account
Bridge funding — allows Govt to draw from CFI between Budget presentation and approval. Normally 2 months (election year: more)
Lame-Duck Session
Last session of outgoing LS before newly elected one meets. Lame-ducks = defeated members
Ordinance (Art. 123)
President promulgates when Parliament NOT in session. Max life: 6 months + 6 weeks (from reassembly). DC Wadhwa Case: Cannot be issued repeatedly
💰 Types of Bills — Key Differences
BILL TYPEKEY FEATURES
Money Bill (Art. 110)Only LS origin; RS can only recommend (14 days); Speaker's certificate FINAL; No Joint Sitting
Financial Bill A (Art. 117)Intro in LS only; President's recommendation needed; RS can amend/reject
Financial Bill BCan be intro in either House; RS can amend/reject
Ordinary BillEither House; RS can reject → Joint Sitting
Joint Sitting (Art. 108)3 actual: Dowry (1961), Banking (1978), POTA (2002). Presided by Speaker of LS
🏦 Three Funds of India
FUNDART.DESCRIPTION
Consolidated Fund of India (CFI)266All revenues + loans + repayments. Requires Parliament appropriation to withdraw. Public purse
Public Account of India266All other public money. In hands of executive — no Parliament vote needed
Contingency Fund267Urgent/unforeseen expenditure. In hands of President (run by President + Finance Secretary)
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗President's EC: Elected MPs + Elected MLAs
VP's EC: ALL MPs (elected + nominated), No MLAs
✗President resigns to VP | VP resigns to President
✗RS removal: initiated only in RS (not LS)
Impeachment: initiated in either House
✗RS Chairman CANNOT certify Money Bill
Only LS Speaker can certify
✗Qualified Veto exists only in USA, NOT India
✗Zero Hour = Indian invention | Not mentioned in Constitution
✅ RAPID RECALL
★President = 1st Citizen | VP = 2nd | PM = 3rd
★Pardoning: President can pardon death sentence; Governor CANNOT
★LS strength: Max 550, Current 543
★RS strength: Max 250, Current 245 | Permanent body
★First AG: M.C. Setalvad (1950–1963)
★Money Bill — RS: 14 days to recommend; else deemed passed
★3 actual Joint Sittings: 1961, 1978, 2002
★Ordinance max life: 6 months + 6 weeks
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st Speaker of LS → G.V. Mavalankar
➤1st Tribal Speaker → P.A. Sangma
➤1st Female Speaker → Meira Kumar
➤1st RS Chairman → Dr. S. Radhakrishnan
➤1st RS Deputy Chairman → S.V. Krishnamoorthy Rao
➤1st Female RS Dy. Chairman → Smt. Violet Alva
➤1st Leader of Opposition (LS) → Y.B. Chavan (1977)
➤NITI Aayog replaced Planning Commission → 2015
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Who is the first citizen and constitutional head of India?
President of India
Q2
How is the President of India elected?
Indirectly — by Electoral College (STV + Secret Ballot)
Q3
Do nominated MPs participate in Presidential election?
No — NOT in President's election
Q4
Who administers the oath of office to the President?
Chief Justice of India (CJI)
Q5
To whom does the President submit resignation?
Vice President of India
Q6
Which court has final say on Presidential election disputes?
Supreme Court of India
Q7
What is the ground for impeachment of the President?
Violation of the Constitution
Q8
Which pardoning power completely removes both punishment and judgement?
Pardon
Q9
Which pardoning power gives a temporary stay of execution?
Reprieve
Q10
Can the Governor pardon a death sentence?
No — Only President can (Art. 72)
Q11
Which veto power exists in USA but NOT in India?
Qualified Veto
Q12
Which veto allows the President to take no action indefinitely?
Pocket Veto
Q13
Who is the second citizen of India?
Vice President of India
Q14
Who administers oath to the Vice President?
President of India
Q15
VP removal can be initiated only in which House?
Rajya Sabha only
Q16
VP is qualified for election to which House?
Rajya Sabha (not Lok Sabha)
Q17
VP acts as President during vacancy for maximum how long?
6 Months
Q18
Who is the ex-officio Chairman of Rajya Sabha?
Vice President of India
Q19
Total ministers including PM must not exceed what % of LS strength?
15% (91st CAA, 2003)
Q20
Who is the highest law officer of India?
Attorney General of India
Q21
Who was the first and longest serving Attorney General of India?
M.C. Setalvad (1950–1963)
Q22
What is the maximum strength of Rajya Sabha?
250 (238 elected + 12 nominated)
Q23
What is the current strength of Lok Sabha?
543
Q24
Minimum age for Lok Sabha member?
25 Years
Q25
Minimum age for Rajya Sabha member?
30 Years
Q26
Who was the first Speaker of Lok Sabha?
G.V. Mavalankar
Q27
Who was the first female Speaker of Lok Sabha?
Meira Kumar
Q28
Which is an Indian Parliamentary invention?
Zero Hour
Q29
How many members' support needed for Adjournment Motion?
50+ Members
Q30
Policy Cut Motion reduces demand by how much?
₹1 (Re. 1)
Q31
Who certifies a Money Bill?
Speaker of Lok Sabha
Q32
Can Money Bill be introduced in Rajya Sabha?
No — Only in Lok Sabha
Q33
How many days does RS get to return a Money Bill?
14 Days
Q34
How many actual Joint Sittings have taken place?
3 (1961, 1978, 2002)
Q35
Who presides over Joint Sitting?
Speaker of Lok Sabha
Q36
What is the maximum life of an Ordinance?
6 months + 6 weeks
Q37
Which fund requires Parliament's approval before withdrawal?
Consolidated Fund of India (CFI)
Q38
Contingency Fund of India is under whose control?
President of India
Q39
What is the minimum quorum to transact business in Parliament?
1/10 of total House membership
Q40
NITI Aayog replaced which body, in which year?
Planning Commission — 2015
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 07 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 07 of 14
Chapter 08 Indian Judiciary — SC + HCs + PIL
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Indian Judiciary
Supreme Court, High Courts & PIL
📖 Chapter 08
⚖️ Guardian of Constitution
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Supreme Court  ●  Jurisdiction  ●  Collegium  ●  High Courts  ●  PIL  ●  Judicial Review  ●  40 MCQs
⚖️ PART A : SUPREME COURT OF INDIA — Arts. 124–147 Apex Court
📌 Key Facts — Supreme Court
  • Constituted on 28th January 1950 — replaced Federal Court of India
  • Maximum strength: 34 judges (1 CJI + 33 others)
  • Appointed by: President of India
  • Seat: Delhi — CJI can declare other seat (with President's permission)
  • Resignation: Letter submitted to President
  • Art. 141: Law declared by SC is binding on all courts in India
  • Art. 129: SC is a Court of Record — can punish for contempt
