  • Former SC judge CANNOT practise law anywhere in India
🗑️ Qualifications & Removal of SC Judge
  • Citizen of India AND one of: HC Judge for 5 years OR HC Advocate for 10 years OR Distinguished Jurist (in President's opinion)
  • Grounds for removal: Proven misbehaviour or incapacity
  • Process: President's order after address by each House of Parliament
  • Majority: ⭐ Special Majority = majority of total membership + ⅔ of members present & voting
📜 Historical Evolution
  • 1773 — Regulating Act: SC at Calcutta
  • 1800, 1823 — SC at Madras & Bombay (King George III)
  • 1861 — Indian HC Act: SC abolished; HCs established
  • 1937 — Federal Court (GoI Act 1935)
  • 1950 — Supreme Court of India constituted
🔭 PART B : JURISDICTION OF SUPREME COURT — 7 Types Most Asked!
📜 Original Jurisdiction
Art. 131

Exclusively settles disputes between Union vs State(s) or State vs State. No other court can deal with such disputes.
🏛️ Appellate Jurisdiction
Arts. 132–134

Hears appeals from HCs in:
• Constitutional matters (Art. 132)
• Civil matters — property, marriage, divorce (Art. 133)
• Criminal matters (Art. 134)
🤝 Advisory Jurisdiction
Art. 143

Advises President on matters of public importance.
• Pre-constitutional matters → SC advice mandatory (but NOT binding)
• General importance → NOT binding
• SC may refuse to give opinion
📜 Writ Jurisdiction
Art. 32

Issues 5 writs to protect Fundamental Rights:
Habeas Corpus, Mandamus, Prohibition, Certiorari, Quo Warranto
SC CANNOT refuse to issue writs
⚡ Special Leave to Appeal
Art. 136

SC can grant special leave to appeal from any judgement of any court in India.
Discretionary power — NOT a right. SC can refuse.
🔄 Review & Enlargement
Arts. 137–138

• Art. 137: SC can review its own judgements
• Art. 138: Parliament can enlarge SC's jurisdiction by law
👥 PART C : COLLEGIUM SYSTEM — 4 Judges Cases ⭐ Very Important!
1st Case
First Judges Case CJI's role was purely consultative — final decision rested with Council of Ministers
2nd Case
1993
Second Judges Case (1993) CJI's opinion MUST be followed by President. No SC/HC appointment without CJI's concurrence. ⭐ Collegium Born
3rd Case
1998
Third Judges Case (1998) 'Consultation' = CJI must consult collegium of 4 senior-most SC judges. Sole CJI opinion alone is NOT sufficient
4th Case
2015
Fourth Judges Case (2015) — NJAC Struck Down 99th CAA & NJAC Act declared unconstitutional & void. Would affect independence of judiciary. Collegium system restored.
⚠️ CJI Convention Broken Twice: 1973 — Justice A.N. Ray appointed (superseding 3 senior judges)  |  1975 — Justice M.H. Beg appointed (superseding Justice H.R. Khanna)
🏛️ PART D : HIGH COURTS OF INDIA — Arts. 214–231 25 HCs
📌 Key Facts — High Courts
  • First HCs in 1862: Calcutta, Bombay, Madras
  • 4th HC in 1866: Allahabad
  • Currently 25 High Courts in India
  • Art. 215: Every HC is a Court of Record
  • Art. 214: Every State shall have a HC
  • 7th Amendment 1956: Common HC for 2+ states allowed
  • Only Delhi UT has a separate HC (since 1966)
  • J&K and Ladakh share a common HC
  • Art. 222: President can transfer judges (after consulting CJI)
  • No HC judge has ever been impeached in India
🗺️ HCs with Multi-State Jurisdiction
HIGH COURT STATES / UTs COVERED
Bombay HCMaharashtra, Goa, D&NH and DD
Guwahati HCAssam, Nagaland, Mizoram & Arunachal Pradesh
Punjab & Haryana HCPunjab, Haryana, Chandigarh
Calcutta HCWest Bengal & A&N Islands
Tamil Nadu HCTamil Nadu, Puducherry
Kerala HCKerala, Lakshadweep
⚖️ SC (Art. 32) vs HC (Art. 226) — Writ Powers
Jurisdiction
SC = Original but NOT exclusive | HC = Exclusive & original
Scope
SC = Only FRs | HC = FRs + legal rights + constitutional + customary rights
Can refuse?
SC = CANNOT refuse (mandatory duty) | HC = May refuse (discretionary)
Territory
SC = Wider (all India) | HC = Narrower (within state)
🏛️ HC Judge — Qualifications & Removal
Qualification
Indian citizen + Judicial office for 10 years OR HC Advocate for 10 years
Key Difference
No minimum age for HC judge (unlike SC) | No provision for distinguished jurist
Oath taken before
Governor of the state (unlike SC judges — oath before President)
Removal motion
100 LS members or 50 RS members sign → 3-member committee investigates
📣 PART E : PUBLIC INTEREST LITIGATION (PIL) — Judicial Activism Social Action
📌 PIL — Key Facts
  • Also called Social Action Litigation (SAL)
  • Term taken from American law
  • Any public-spirited citizen can file on behalf of deprived
  • Courts even took up cases based on newspaper reports & postal complaints
  • ⭐ KEY Justice Krishna Iyer first introduced PIL concept in India (1976)
  • Justice P.N. Bhagwati marked a new phase in PIL (S.P. Gupta case)
PIL vs Normal Law:
Normal: Only aggrieved party can approach court
PIL: Any bonafide public member can file on behalf of deprived
📅 Evolution of PIL
  • 1976 — Mumbai Kamagar Sabha vs. Abdul Thai: Justice Krishna Iyer first introduced PIL concept
  • 1979 — Hussainara Khatoon vs. State of Bihar: ⭐ First publicly known PIL — Released 40,000+ undertrial prisoners; established right to speedy trial
  • S.P. Gupta vs. Union of India: Any bonafide public member can use writ jurisdiction (Art. 32/226)
  • M.C. Mehta vs. Union of India: PIL on Ganga pollution
  • Vishaka vs. State of Rajasthan: Sexual harassment violates Arts. 14, 15, 16, 21 → workplace guidelines
📚 PART F : LANDMARK CASES — Quick Reference All Important!
Hussainara Khatoon vs State of Bihar (1979)
First publicly known PIL. Released 40,000+ undertrial prisoners. Established right to speedy trial
S.P. Gupta vs Union of India
Any bonafide public member can use writ jurisdiction (Art. 32/226). PIL became tool for enforcing public duties
Keshavananda Bharati Case (1973)
Established Basic Structure Doctrine. Parliament cannot amend basic structure. 13-judge bench
Second Judges Case (1993)
CJI's opinion binding on President for judicial appointments. Collegium system born
Third Judges Case (1998)
Consultation = collegium of 4 senior-most SC judges. Sole CJI opinion insufficient
Fourth Judges Case (2015)
99th CAA & NJAC Act declared unconstitutional. Collegium system restored
M.C. Mehta vs Union of India
PIL on Ganga water pollution. Expanded locus standi in environmental cases
Vishaka vs State of Rajasthan
Sexual harassment violates Arts. 14, 15, 16, 21. Led to workplace harassment guidelines
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗SC judge oath → President
HC judge oath → Governor
✗SC judge CANNOT practice anywhere in India after retirement
HC judge CAN practice (except in same HC)
✗Advisory opinion (Art. 143) = SC advice NOT binding on President
✗SC writ = CANNOT refuse
HC writ = MAY refuse (discretionary)
✗NJAC: 99th CAA 2014 → struck down 2015 (4th Judges Case)
✗Removal: 100 LS members OR 50 RS members sign motion for HC judge
✅ RAPID RECALL
★SC constituted → 28 January 1950
★SC max strength = 34 judges (1 CJI + 33)
★Art. 141 = SC law binding on all courts in India
★Art. 136 = Special Leave to Appeal (Discretionary)
★Currently 25 High Courts in India
★First publicly known PIL = Hussainara Khatoon 1979
★PIL introduced by Justice Krishna Iyer (1976)
★Judicial Review = NOT explicitly in Constitution — implied
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤First SC at Calcutta → Regulating Act 1773
➤Federal Court established → 1937 (GoI Act 1935)
➤First HCs (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras) → 1862
➤4th HC (Allahabad) → 1866
➤PIL concept introduced by → Justice Krishna Iyer (1976)
➤CJI convention broken → 1973 (A.N. Ray) & 1975 (M.H. Beg)
➤AIJS proposed via → Art. 312 (RS ⅔ majority needed)
➤Delhi UT got separate HC → 1966
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
On which date was the Supreme Court of India constituted?
28th January 1950
Q2
What is the maximum strength of the Supreme Court?
34 judges (1 CJI + 33 others)
Q3
Who appoints judges of the Supreme Court?
President of India
Q4
What is the minimum experience needed for a HC judge to be appointed to SC?
5 years as HC judge
Q5
What is the minimum advocacy experience at HC to be appointed SC judge?
10 years as HC Advocate
Q6
On what grounds can a SC judge be removed?
Proven misbehaviour or incapacity
Q7
Which Article states that SC law is binding on all courts in India?
Article 141
Q8
Can a retired SC judge practise law in India?
No — Cannot practise anywhere in India
Q9
Which jurisdiction exclusively settles Union vs State disputes?
Original Jurisdiction (Art. 131)
Q10
Is the SC's advisory opinion (Art. 143) binding on the President?
No — NOT binding
Q11
Under which Article can the SC grant Special Leave to Appeal?
Article 136
Q12
Can the Supreme Court review its own judgements?
Yes — Article 137
Q13
Which case established the collegium system for judicial appointments?
Second Judges Case, 1993
Q14
Third Judges Case (1998) said collegium = CJI + how many judges?
4 senior-most SC judges
Q15
NJAC was declared unconstitutional in which case?
Fourth Judges Case, 2015
Q16
NJAC was introduced by which Constitutional Amendment?
99th CAA, 2014
Q17
When did the CJI convention get broken for the first time?
1973 — Justice A.N. Ray appointed
Q18
How many High Courts are there in India currently?
25 High Courts
Q19
In which year were the first High Courts established in India?
1862 (Calcutta, Bombay, Madras)
Q20
Which is the only UT with its own separate High Court?
Delhi (since 1966)
Q21
Before whom does an HC judge take oath?
Governor of the State
Q22
Under which Article can HC issue writs for both FRs and legal rights?
Article 226
Q23
Can the HC refuse to issue a writ?
Yes — It is discretionary
Q24
How many LS members needed to initiate HC judge removal motion?
100 Members (LS) or 50 (RS)
Q25
Has any HC judge ever been impeached in India?
No — Never impeached
Q26
Who first introduced the concept of PIL in India?
Justice Krishna Iyer (1976)
Q27
What is PIL also known as?
Social Action Litigation (SAL)
Q28
Which is the first publicly known PIL case in India?
Hussainara Khatoon vs Bihar, 1979
Q29
Hussainara Khatoon case resulted in release of how many prisoners?
40,000+ undertrial prisoners
Q30
PIL term was borrowed from which country's law?
American (USA) Law
Q31
M.C. Mehta PIL was filed against pollution of which river?
Ganga River
Q32
Vishaka case established guidelines against what?
Sexual Harassment at Workplace
Q33
'Judicial Review' is mentioned where in the Constitution?
Nowhere — It is implied (not explicit)
Q34
Under which Article can Parliament create All India Judicial Service?
Article 312
Q35
Bombay High Court has jurisdiction over which states/UTs?
Maharashtra, Goa, D&NH and DD
Q36
Guwahati High Court covers which states?
Assam, Nagaland, Mizoram, Arunachal Pradesh
Q37
Which article allows President to transfer HC judges?
Article 222
Q38
Doctrine of 'stare decisis' means what in judiciary?
Previous decisions binding on later judges in similar cases
Q39
Under Article 127, who can appoint Ad Hoc judges to the SC?
Chief Justice of India (CJI)
Q40
Which case established that the Right to Property is NOT part of Basic Structure?
Keshavananda Bharati Case, 1973
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 08 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 08 of 14
Chapter 09 The States — Governor · CM · Legislature
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
The States — Governor, CM
State Legislature (Part VI, Arts. 152–237)
📖 Chapter 09
🏛️ Part VI
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Governor  ●  Powers  ●  CM  ●  Vidhan Sabha  ●  Vidhan Parishad  ●  Bills  ●  40 MCQs
🏛️ PART A : GOVERNOR OF STATE — Arts. 153–167 Constitutional Head
📌 Appointment & Basic Facts
  • Appointed by President by warrant (Art. 155)
  • Governor = Constitutional (nominal) head of State + Agent of Central Govt
  • 7th CAA 1956: Same person can be Governor of two or more States
  • Qualifications: Indian citizen + age ≥ 35 years + not hold office of profit + not a legislator
  • Term: 5 years — holds office during pleasure of the President
  • Can be appointed more than once — no bar on re-appointment
  • Resignation: Addressed to President
  • Oath administered by: Chief Justice of the HC (or senior-most judge)
💡 Why Governor is Appointed (NOT Elected)?
  • Saves country from another election on personal issues
  • Elected Governor might feel superior to CM → friction
  • Cost & machinery of elections don't match Governor's limited powers
  • Risk of a second-rate party functionary getting elected
  • Through appointed Governor, Union can maintain control over States
⚙️ Executive Powers
  • Appoints Chief Minister, Council of Ministers, Advocate General, State PSC members
  • Ministers & Advocate General hold office during pleasure of Governor
  • State PSC members: removed only by President (on SC report)
  • Art. 333: Can nominate one Anglo-Indian member to Legislative Assembly
  • No power to appoint HC judges — but entitled to be consulted
📜 Legislative Powers
  • Governor is a part of State Legislature
  • Summons, prorogues & dissolves State Assembly
  • Art. 200: Can refer any bill to President's approval
  • Max gap between two sessions: 6 months (Art. 174)
  • Art. 176: Addresses Legislature at commencement of 1st session after general elections
⚖️ Judicial & Emergency
  • Art. 161: Can grant pardons, reprieves, respites, remissions
  • CANNOT pardon: Death sentence (only President) | Martial Court sentences
  • No emergency powers against external aggression
  • Can report to President if State Govt cannot function → basis for President's Rule (Art. 356)
🚫 Governor's Veto Power (Art. 200) & Ordinance (Art. 213)
⚡ 4 Options for Governor on Bills
  • Give assent → Bill becomes law
  • Withhold assent → Bill fails
  • Return with message (NOT Money Bill) → Legislature may reconsider; must give assent to reconsidered bill
  • Reserve for President → Compulsory if law would derogate HC powers
📋 Ordinance Powers (Art. 213)
  • Only when Legislature NOT in session
  • Must be on aid and advice of CoM
  • Ceases after 6 weeks from date of re-assembly
  • Governor CAN withdraw Ordinance at any time
  • Scope: Lists II & III of 7th Schedule only
🔒 Discretionary Functions of Governor (State-specific)
AssamDetermine royalty from mineral licences payable to District Council
NagalandSpecial responsibility regarding law and order
ManipurProper functioning of Hill Areas Committee of Legislative Assembly
Maharashtra/GujaratSpecial development responsibility for Vidarbha and Saurashtra
SikkimPeace and equitable arrangement for social & economic advancement
Dismiss MinistersCan dismiss individual Minister / CoM (when lost confidence & doesn't dissolve Assembly)
🏛️ PART B : CHIEF MINISTER & COUNCIL OF MINISTERS — Arts. 163–167 State Executive
⚙️ Key Constitutional Provisions
  • Art. 164: CM appointed by Governor; other ministers appointed on CM's advice
  • Any person can be appointed Minister → must join Legislature within 6 months
  • Governor administers oaths of office and secrecy
  • CoM collectively responsible to Legislative Assembly
  • CoM individually responsible to Governor
  • Art. 163: CoM aids & advises Governor — advice NOT binding in discretionary matters
  • Art. 166: All state executive actions in name of Governor
  • Art. 167: CM must furnish information to the Governor
⚖️ Advocate General for State (Art. 165)
  • State equivalent of Attorney General of India
  • Appointed by Governor — holds office during Governor's pleasure
  • Must be qualified to be a HC Judge
  • Remuneration determined by Governor
  • Right to speak and take part in both Houses of State Legislature
  • NO right to vote
💡 Comparison: AG of India vs Advocate General
AG India → appointed by President | Advocate General → appointed by Governor
🏛️ PART C : STATE LEGISLATURE — Vidhan Sabha & Vidhan Parishad Arts. 168–212
🏛️ BICAMERAL STATES (6) — Trick: K A B U T M
K
Karnataka
A
Andhra Pradesh
B
Bihar
U
Uttar Pradesh
T
Telangana
M
Maharashtra
🟢 VIDHAN SABHA (Legislative Assembly)
500
Maximum strength (not more than 500 MLAs)
60
Minimum strength (not less than 60 MLAs)
30
Special minimum: Sikkim, Goa, Arunachal Pradesh (30 members)
5 yrs
Term — from date of 1st meeting; can be dissolved earlier
25
Minimum age for MLA
  • Directly elected by adult suffrage — FPTP system
  • Governor can nominate 1 Anglo-Indian member
  • Speaker & Deputy Speaker elected by Assembly members (Art. 178)
  • Quorum: 10% of total membership
🔴 VIDHAN PARISHAD (Legislative Council)
1/3rd
Maximum = 1/3rd of LA membership
40
Minimum = not less than 40 members
6 yrs
Member term | 1/3rd retire every 2 years
30
Minimum age for MLC
∞
Permanent body — NOT dissolved
  • Creation/Abolition: Parliament by simple majority (after LA special majority resolution)
  • 5/6 elected indirectly | 1/6 nominated by Governor
  • Has Chairman & Deputy Chairman (Art. 182)
  • NO joint sitting provision in State Legislature
📊 Vidhan Parishad — How Members are Chosen
CATEGORYPROPORTION
Elected by Local Bodies (municipalities, district boards etc.)1/3rd
Elected by Graduates (3 years standing residing in state)1/12th
Elected by Teachers (secondary schools & higher — 3+ years)1/12th
Elected by Members of Legislative Assembly (from non-members)1/3rd
Nominated by Governor — Literature, Science, Art, Cooperative Movement, Social Service (LiSAS + Cooperative)1/6th
⚠️ Key Difference: Governor nominates to LC with 'Cooperative Movement' in the list. President's RS nominations do NOT include 'Cooperative Movement'
💰 PART D : BILLS & FINANCIAL PROCEDURE — State Legislature Arts. 196–213
💰 Money Bills (Arts. 198–199)
✅Introduced only in Legislative Assembly (NOT Council)
📋Sent to LC for recommendations only — cannot reject
⏰LC must return within 14 days — else deemed passed
⭐Art. 207: Governor's prior recommendation mandatory for Money Bills
📜 Ordinary Bills — LC Power & Limitation
⏳LC can only delay ordinary bills by max 4 months total
📌1st instance: LC delays by 3 months
📌2nd referral: LC can hold for 1 month only
⚠️If Bill originates in LC & LA disagrees → Bill lapses (no joint sitting)
🚫NO provision for Joint Sitting in State Legislature
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗Governor oath → CJ of HC
President oath → CJI
✗Governor resigns to President
VP resigns to President
President resigns to VP
✗Governor CANNOT pardon death sentence — only President can
✗LC nomination: includes Cooperative Movement
RS nomination: does NOT include Cooperative
✗Bicameral states = 6 (KABUTM)
NOT 7 or 8
✗NO Joint Sitting in State Legislature (unlike Parliament)
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Governor = Constitutional head + Agent of Centre
★7th CAA 1956 = same person as Governor of 2+ states
★Governor cannot pardon death sentence — President can
★Vidhan Sabha: 60–500 members | 5-year term
★Vidhan Parishad: Min 40, Max 1/3rd of LA | Permanent body
★LC delay: Max 3 months (1st) + 1 month (2nd) = 4 months total
★MLA minimum age = 25 yrs | MLC = 30 yrs
★Quorum in State Legislature = 10% of total membership
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤Part VI covers → Arts. 152–237
➤Governor appointed by → President (Art. 155)
➤6 Bicameral States → K A B U T M
➤State PSC members removed only by → President
➤Advocate General → appointed by Governor
➤Sikkim, Goa, Arunachal Pradesh min LA strength → 30 members
➤Mizoram LA members → 40 | Nagaland → 46
➤Art. 192: Disqualification decision → Governor + Election Commission opinion
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Who appoints the Governor of a State?
President of India (Art. 155)
Q2
What is the minimum age required to become Governor?
35 Years
Q3
How long is the normal term of a Governor?
5 Years (holds during President's pleasure)
Q4
To whom does the Governor submit resignation?
President of India
Q5
Who administers the oath to the Governor?
Chief Justice of the High Court
Q6
Which CAA allowed one person to be Governor of two states?
7th CAA, 1956
Q7
Can the Governor pardon a death sentence?
No — Only President can
Q8
How many Anglo-Indian members can Governor nominate to Legislative Assembly?
One (Art. 333)
Q9
What is the maximum gap between two sessions of State Legislature?
6 Months (Art. 174)
Q10
Governor can refer any bill to whom for approval? (Art. 200)
President of India
Q11
How long does an Ordinance of Governor remain effective after Legislature reassembles?
6 Weeks
Q12
In discretionary matters, is the Governor bound by CoM advice?
No — NOT binding (Art. 163)
Q13
Which state has special provision for Governor's responsibility in law & order?
Nagaland
Q14
Who appoints the Chief Minister of a State?
Governor (Art. 164)
Q15
How long can a non-legislator minister remain without joining Legislature?
6 Months
Q16
State CoM is collectively responsible to which body?
Legislative Assembly
Q17
Who appoints the Advocate General for a State?
Governor of the State (Art. 165)
Q18
Can the Advocate General vote in State Legislature proceedings?
No — Can speak but NOT vote
Q19
How many bicameral states are there in India?
6 States (KABUTM)
Q20
Name the 6 bicameral states of India.
Karnataka, AP, Bihar, UP, Telangana, Maharashtra
Q21
What is the maximum strength of Legislative Assembly?
500 Members
Q22
What is the minimum strength of Legislative Assembly?
60 Members (30 for Sikkim, Goa, Arunachal)
Q23
What is the term of Vidhan Sabha?
5 Years from date of first meeting
Q24
Minimum age for MLA?
25 Years
Q25
Minimum age for MLC (Vidhan Parishad member)?
30 Years
Q26
What is the quorum for State Legislature?
10% of total membership
Q27
What is the maximum strength of Vidhan Parishad?
1/3rd of LA strength
Q28
What is the minimum strength of Legislative Council?
40 Members
Q29
Is Vidhan Parishad a permanent body?
Yes — NOT dissolved
Q30
How many members retire every 2 years from Vidhan Parishad?
1/3rd of total members
Q31
What proportion of LC members is nominated by Governor?
1/6th (Literature, Science, Art, Cooperative, Social Service)
Q32
What proportion of LC members is elected by Local Bodies?
1/3rd
Q33
Can Money Bills be introduced in Vidhan Parishad?
No — Only in Vidhan Sabha
Q34
How many days does LC get to return a Money Bill?
14 Days
Q35
What is the maximum delay LC can impose on an Ordinary Bill?
4 Months (3 + 1 months)
Q36
Is there a provision for Joint Sitting in State Legislature?
No — NO Joint Sitting in States
Q37
If a Bill originated in LC is rejected by LA, what happens?
Bill Lapses (no joint sitting)
Q38
Who decides disqualification of state legislators? (Art. 192)
Governor + Election Commission opinion
Q39
Who can remove State PSC members?
Only President (on SC report)
Q40
Creation/abolition of LC by Parliament requires what from the State LA?
Special majority resolution (2/3rd majority)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 09 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 09 of 14
Chapter 10 Union Territories — Part VIII
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Union Territories
Part VIII — Articles 239 to 242
📖 Chapter 10
🗺️ 8 Union Territories
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Art. 239  ●  Types of Admins  ●  Delhi (Art. 239AA)  ●  HC Jurisdictions  ●  40 MCQs
🗺️ PART A : UNION TERRITORIES — Introduction & Background Part VIII
📌 Key Framework
  • Covered under Part VIII — Articles 239 to 242
  • UTs governed directly by Central Government through the President
  • 7th CAA, 1956 — Landmark reorganisation; abolished Parts A, B, C, D classification
  • Parts C and D States → became unified category: "Union Territories"
  • Part C States administered through Chief Commissioner/Lt. Governor as President's agent
💡 Why UTs Were Created?
  • Too small geographically/financially for independent statehood (e.g., Lakshadweep)
  • Strategically sensitive locations (e.g., Andaman & Nicobar Islands)
  • Cultural/historical distinctness — Puducherry (French), Daman & Diu (Portuguese)
  • Political/administrative significance — Delhi (national capital), Chandigarh (shared capital of Punjab & Haryana)
🗺️ Current 8 Union Territories of India — Type of Administrator
Delhi (NCT)
Lieutenant Governor
Puducherry
Lieutenant Governor
J&K
Lieutenant Governor
Andaman & Nicobar
Lieutenant Governor
Ladakh
Lieutenant Governor
Chandigarh
Administrator
Lakshadweep
Administrator
D&NH & D&D
Administrator
⭐ TRICK: 5 UTs have Lt. Governor (Delhi, Puducherry, J&K, A&N Islands, Ladakh) | 3 UTs have Administrator (Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, D&NH & D&D)
📜 PART B : KEY ARTICLES — 239 to 241 Article-wise

Art. 239 — Administration of UTs

  • Every UT administered by President through an appointed administrator
  • President assigns designation — Administrator or Lt. Governor
  • Art. 239(2): President may appoint Governor of adjoining State as UT administrator
  • In such case, Governor acts independently of his CoM for UT matters

Art. 239A — Legislature for Certain UTs

  • Added by 14th CAA, 1962
  • Empowers Parliament to create a Legislature (elected/partly-elected/partly-nominated)
  • Also allows creation of Council of Ministers for certain UTs

Art. 239AB — Failure of Constitutional Machinery

  • Inserted by 69th CAA, 1991 alongside Art. 239AA
  • If governance of NCT Delhi cannot be carried out per Art. 239AA
  • President may suspend provisions and make alternate arrangements
  • Analogous to President's Rule (Art. 356) for States

Art. 239B — Ordinance Power for UTs

  • Ordinance promulgated during recess of UT Legislature
  • Requires prior approval/instructions from President
  • Ordinance lapses if not approved by Legislature within stipulated period

Art. 240 — President's Power to Make Regulations

  • President can make regulations for peace, progress & good governance of certain UTs
  • Applies to: Andaman & Nicobar, Lakshadweep, D&NH & D&D, Chandigarh, Puducherry (only when legislature dissolved/suspended)
  • Such regulations have same effect as Acts of Parliament
  • President CANNOT exercise this power once UT Legislature is constituted and functioning

Art. 241 — High Courts for UTs ⭐

  • Parliament can constitute a HC for a UT or declare existing court as HC
  • Delhi HC = only UT with its own exclusive High Court
  • Amendments to Art. 241 require 2/3rd majority of House present & voting
  • NDMC vs State of Punjab (1997): UTs do NOT enjoy same legislative autonomy as States
🏙️ PART C : SPECIAL PROVISIONS FOR DELHI — Art. 239AA ⭐ 69th CAA 1991

🏙️ Article 239AA — National Capital Territory (NCT) of Delhi

Added by
69th CAA, 1991
Administrator
Lieutenant Governor (LG)
LA Seats
70 Members (Direct Election)
Max Ministers (incl. CM)
< 10% of MLAs = < 7 members
CM Appointed by
President (unlike States — by Governor)
LA History
1952 → abolished 1986 → reconstituted 1991
⛔ 3 Subjects EXCLUDED from Delhi's Legislative Jurisdiction:
🚔 Public Order
👮 Police
🏠 Land
On these 3 subjects, Delhi functions under Union List — LG/Centre controls. Parliament has OVERRIDING powers on all Delhi matters.
⚖️ Landmark Case: Govt. of NCT of Delhi v. Union of India (2018)
  • ⭐ KEY SC clarified: LG is bound by the aid and advice of the elected Delhi Government (CoM)
  • Exceptions: Public Order, Police, Land → LG/Centre controls these
  • Judgment upheld democratic governance and cooperative federalism
⚖️ PART D : HIGH COURT JURISDICTIONS FOR UTs Art. 241
UNION TERRITORYHIGH COURT HAVING JURISDICTION
🏙️ Delhi (NCT)Delhi High Court — ⭐ Only UT with exclusive HC
PuducherryMadras High Court
ChandigarhPunjab & Haryana High Court
LakshadweepKerala High Court
Dadra & Nagar Haveli + Daman & DiuBombay High Court
Andaman & Nicobar IslandsCalcutta High Court
LadakhJammu & Kashmir and Ladakh High Court
Jammu & KashmirJammu & Kashmir and Ladakh High Court
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗5 UTs: Lt. Governor (Delhi, Puducherry, J&K, A&N, Ladakh)
3 UTs: Administrator (Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, D&NH&D&D)
✗Delhi CM appointed by President (NOT Governor like States)
✗Art. 239AA = Delhi Special Provisions (69th CAA 1991)
Art. 239A = Legislature for UTs (14th CAA 1962)
✗Sarkaria Commission set up BEFORE 69th CAA to examine Delhi
✗Puducherry → Madras HC (NOT Kerala HC)
Lakshadweep → Kerala HC
✗Parliament has OVERRIDING powers on ALL Delhi matters
✅ RAPID RECALL
★UTs covered under Part VIII — Arts. 239–242
★7th CAA 1956 = Created UT category (abolished Parts A,B,C,D)
★Art. 239AA (Delhi) added by 69th CAA, 1991
★Delhi LA = 70 seats | Ministers < 7 (incl. CM)
★Delhi excluded subjects: Public Order, Police, Land
★2018 SC ruling: LG bound by Delhi CoM's advice
★Art. 239AB = Failure of constitutional machinery in Delhi
★Only Delhi has its own exclusive HC among all UTs
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤UTs created by → 7th CAA, 1956
➤Legislature for UTs → Art. 239A (14th CAA, 1962)
➤Delhi as NCT → Art. 239AA (69th CAA, 1991)
➤Only UT with exclusive HC → Delhi (since 1966)
➤Chandigarh = Shared capital of Punjab & Haryana
➤Puducherry = French heritage | Daman & Diu = Portuguese
➤J&K made UT → 31 October 2019 (Jammu & Kashmir Reorganisation Act)
➤D&NH + D&D merged into single UT → 2020
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Union Territories are covered under which Part of the Constitution?
Part VIII (Arts. 239–242)
Q2
Which Amendment Act created the category of Union Territories?
7th CAA, 1956
Q3
How many Union Territories are there in India currently?
8 Union Territories
Q4
UTs are governed directly by whom?
Central Government through President
Q5
Under which Article is UT administration governed?
Article 239
Q6
How many UTs have a Lieutenant Governor?
5 UTs (Delhi, Puducherry, J&K, A&N, Ladakh)
Q7
How many UTs have an Administrator (not Lt. Governor)?
3 UTs (Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, D&NH & D&D)
Q8
Which Article empowers Parliament to create Legislatures for UTs?
Article 239A (14th CAA, 1962)
Q9
Which Article contains special provisions for Delhi?
Article 239AA (69th CAA, 1991)
Q10
How many seats does Delhi Legislative Assembly have?
70 Seats
Q11
What is the maximum number of ministers (including CM) in Delhi?
Less than 7 (less than 10% of 70 MLAs)
Q12
Who appoints the Chief Minister of Delhi?
President of India (not LG/Governor)
Q13
Name the 3 subjects excluded from Delhi's Legislative jurisdiction.
Public Order, Police, Land
Q14
What does the 2018 SC ruling say about Delhi's LG?
LG is bound by aid & advice of elected CoM
Q15
Delhi's LA was abolished in which year and reconstituted when?
Abolished 1986 → Reconstituted 1991 (69th CAA)
Q16
Which Article deals with failure of constitutional machinery in Delhi?
Article 239AB
Q17
Which Article gives President power to make regulations for UTs?
Article 240
Q18
For which UTs can President make regulations under Art. 240?
A&N Islands, Lakshadweep, D&NH&D&D, Chandigarh, Puducherry
Q19
Which Article deals with High Courts for Union Territories?
Article 241
Q20
Which is the only UT with its own exclusive High Court?
Delhi (Delhi High Court, since 1966)
Q21
Which High Court has jurisdiction over Chandigarh?
Punjab & Haryana High Court
Q22
Which High Court has jurisdiction over Lakshadweep?
Kerala High Court
Q23
Which High Court has jurisdiction over Puducherry?
Madras High Court
Q24
Which High Court has jurisdiction over Andaman & Nicobar Islands?
Calcutta High Court
Q25
Which High Court covers Dadra & Nagar Haveli and Daman & Diu?
Bombay High Court
Q26
Chandigarh is the shared capital of which two states?
Punjab and Haryana
Q27
Which UT has French colonial heritage?
Puducherry
Q28
Which UT has Portuguese colonial heritage?
Daman & Diu
Q29
J&K was made a UT on which date?
31st October 2019
Q30
D&NH and D&D were merged into one UT in which year?
2020
Q31
Which commission examined administrative reorganisation of Delhi before 69th CAA?
Sarkaria Commission
Q32
Art. 239(2) allows President to appoint whom as UT administrator?
Governor of an adjoining State
Q33
If Governor is appointed UT administrator, does he consult his State CoM?
No — Acts independently of State CoM
Q34
Which UT is strategically sensitive and has Lt. Governor?
Andaman & Nicobar Islands
Q35
Under Art. 239B, who must approve a UT Ordinance?
President of India
Q36
Parliament has overriding powers on Delhi on how many subjects?
All matters (overriding power on everything)
Q37
NDMC vs State of Punjab (1997) established what about UTs?
UTs do NOT have same legislative autonomy as States
Q38
Ladakh has which type of administrator?
Lieutenant Governor
Q39
Which High Court covers both J&K and Ladakh?
J&K and Ladakh High Court
Q40
Which Article 239AB is analogous to for UTs?
Article 356 (President's Rule for States)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 10 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 10 of 14
Chapter 11 Local Governance — 73rd & 74th CAA
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Local Governance
73rd & 74th CAA — Panchayats & Municipalities
📖 Chapter 11
🏘️ Rural + Urban Local Bodies
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ Committees  ●  73rd CAA  ●  11th Schedule  ●  74th CAA  ●  12th Schedule  ●  Comparison  ●  40 MCQs
📋 PART A : EVOLUTION — Key Committees on Panchayati Raj 1957–1988
YEARCOMMITTEEKEY RECOMMENDATIONS
1957
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee
(Community Dev. Programmes)
  • ⭐ FIRST to recommend 3-tier Panchayati Raj: Gram Panchayat → Panchayat Samiti → Zilla Parishad
  • Planning & development at district level | District Collector as ZP Chairman
  • Rajasthan implemented first — Oct 2, 1959 at Nagaur (PM Nehru inaugurated)
1977
Ashok Mehta Committee
(PRI Institutions)
  • Proposed 2-tier system: Zilla Parishad + Mandal Panchayat
  • Recommended constitutional recognition for PRIs
  • Nyaya Panchayat for judicial functions | Compulsory taxation powers
  • Political parties to participate | Minister for Panchayati Raj in state cabinet
1985
G.V.K. Rao Committee
(Rural Development)
  • Zilla Parishad as most important body in democratic decentralisation
  • Post of District Development Commissioner (CEO of ZP) to be created
1986
L.M. Singhvi Committee
(Revitalisation of PRIs)
  • ⭐ KEY Constitutional recognition for PRIs — new chapter in Constitution
  • Financial autonomy for Panchayats | Gram Sabha = embodiment of direct democracy
  • Directly led to 73rd CAA
1988
Thungon Committee
(District Planning)
  • 3-tier system | Fixed tenure of 5 years | Reservations for women
  • District Collector as CEO of Zilla Parishad
1988
Gadgil Committee
(Policy & Programmes)
  • Direct elections at all three levels | Fixed 5-year term
  • Judicial tribunals for PRI election disputes | Recommended constitutionality
🏘️ PART B : 73rd CAA, 1992 — PANCHAYATI RAJ April 24, 1993
Passed
Dec 1992
In Force
24 Apr 1993
Part Added
Part IX (243–243O)
Schedule Added
11th Schedule (29 items)
🗓️ Panchayati Raj Diwas = April 24  |  PM at time: P.V. Narasimha Rao  |  Gram Sabha = body of persons registered in electoral rolls of a village
🏘️ 3-TIER PANCHAYATI RAJ SYSTEM
3
Zilla Parishad
District Level | Indirect election of Chairman
▼
2
Panchayat Samiti / Block
Intermediate Level | Skipped if State pop ≤ 20 lakh
▼
1
Gram Panchayat
Village Level | Direct election of all seats
📋 Key Articles 243A to 243O — Summary
ART.KEY PROVISION
243B3-tier system; States ≤ 20 lakh pop → intermediate tier optional
243DReservation: SC/ST proportional | Women ≥ 1/3rd (seats + Chairpersons)
243E5-year term; if dissolved → elections within 6 months
243FMinimum age for membership = 21 years
243GState may endow powers for economic development; 29 functions in 11th Schedule
243IGovernor appoints State Finance Commission every 5 years (recommendations NOT binding)
243KState Election Commission (SEC) conducts elections; Commissioner appointed by Governor, removed like HC Judge
243MExempt: Nagaland, Meghalaya, Mizoram, tribal/hill areas of Manipur. Delhi (only UT exempt)
243OCourts CANNOT interfere in delimitation of constituencies or allotment of seats
📋 11th Schedule — 29 Functional Items of Panchayats Illustrative — Not Mandatory
1
Agriculture incl. agricultural extension
2
Land improvement, reforms, soil conservation
3
Minor irrigation, water management, watershed
4
Animal husbandry, dairying & poultry
5
Fisheries
6
Social forestry & farm forestry
7
Minor forest produce
8
Small-scale industries incl. food processing
9
Khadi, village & cottage industries
10
Rural housing
11
Drinking water
12
Fuel and fodder
13
Roads, culverts, bridges, ferries, waterways
14
Rural electrification incl. distribution
15
Non-conventional energy sources
16
Poverty alleviation programmes
17
Education incl. primary & secondary schools
18
Technical training & vocational education
19
Adult & non-formal education
20
Libraries
21
Cultural activities
22
Markets and fairs
23
Health & sanitation, hospitals, PHCs
24
Family welfare
25
Women & child development
26
Social welfare incl. handicapped & mentally retarded
27
Welfare of weaker sections — SC & ST
28
Public distribution system
29
Maintenance of community assets
🏙️ PART C : 74th CAA, 1992 — MUNICIPALITIES (Urban) June 1, 1993
Passed
Dec 1992
In Force
June 1, 1993
Part Added
Part IXA (243P–243ZG)
Schedule Added
12th Schedule (18 items)
📜 History: First Municipal Corporation = Madras (1687–88) | Lord Ripon's Resolution 1882 = "Magna Carta of Local Self-Government" | Lord Ripon = Father of Local Self-Government | 65th CAA (Nagar Palika Bill, Rajiv Gandhi) — passed LS but REJECTED by RS (1989)
Nagar Panchayat
Transitional areas (rural → urban)
Municipal Council
Small cities & towns | Head: Chairman/President
Municipal Corporation
Large cities/metro areas | Head: Mayor
ART.KEY PROVISION
243QObligatory for every state: Nagar Panchayat, Municipal Council, or Municipal Corporation. Delhi NOT exempt
243TReservation: SC/ST proportional | OBCs (by state) | Women ≥ 1/3rd
243U5-year term; dissolution → elections within 6 months; <6 months left → not mandatory
243VMinimum age = 21 years
243ZDDistrict Planning Committee — consolidates plans of Panchayats & Municipalities
243ZEMetropolitan Planning Committee — for cities with population > 10 lakh
📋 12th Schedule — 18 Items (Municipalities)

1. Urban planning & town planning

2. Land use & construction regulation

3. Economic & social development plans

4. Roads and bridges

5. Water supply (domestic, industrial)

6. Public health, sanitation, solid waste

7. Fire services

8. Urban forestry, environment & ecology

9. Interests of weaker sections

10. Slum improvement & upgradation

11. Urban poverty alleviation

12. Parks, gardens, playgrounds

13. Cultural, educational & aesthetic aspects

14. Burials, cremations & crematoriums

15. Cattle ponds, prevention of cruelty

16. Vital statistics (births & deaths)

17. Street lighting, parking, bus stops

18. Slaughterhouses & tanneries regulation

⚖️ PART D : PANCHAYATS vs MUNICIPALITIES — Full Comparison ⭐ Most Asked!
PARAMETERPANCHAYATS (Rural)MUNICIPALITIES (Urban)
Constitutional Amendment73rd CAA, 199274th CAA, 1992
Came into ForceApril 24, 1993 (Panchayati Raj Diwas)June 1, 1993
Part in ConstitutionPart IX (Art. 243–243O)Part IXA (Art. 243P–243ZG)
Schedule Added11th Schedule (29 items)12th Schedule (18 items)
Basic Democratic UnitGram SabhaWard Committee
Tiers / Types3-tier: Gram Panchayat → Block → Zilla Parishad3 types: Nagar Panchayat → Municipal Council → Municipal Corp.
Head at Top LevelAdhyaksha (varies by state)Mayor (Municipal Corp.) | Chairman (Municipality)
Elections byState Election CommissionState Election Commission (same body)
Term5 years5 years
Women Reservation≥ 1/3rd seats + Chairpersons≥ 1/3rd seats + Chairpersons
Finance ReviewState Finance Commission (Art. 243I)Same SFC (Art. 243Y)
Min. Membership Age21 years21 years
Exception AreasNagaland, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Hill areas Manipur, DelhiSimilar exclusions apply
Chief Executive—Municipal Commissioner (appointed by State Govt)
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗73rd CAA in force → April 24, 1993
74th CAA in force → June 1, 1993
✗11th Schedule = 29 items (Panchayats)
12th Schedule = 18 items (Municipalities)
✗First 3-tier PR system → Balwant Rai Mehta 1957
Directly led to 73rd CAA → L.M. Singhvi 1986
✗First Municipal Corp → Madras (1687)
NOT Bombay or Calcutta
✗Father of Local Self-Govt → Lord Ripon
Magna Carta of LSG → Lord Ripon's Resolution 1882
✗65th CAA (Nagar Palika) → REJECTED by Rajya Sabha (not Lok Sabha)
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Panchayati Raj Diwas = April 24
★Rajasthan 1st to implement PR → Oct 2, 1959, Nagaur
★Women reservation in Panchayats = ≥ 1/3rd
★State SFC every 5 years — appointed by Governor
★States ≤ 20 lakh pop = no intermediate tier needed
★Metropolitan Planning Committee = cities > 10 lakh pop
★Municipal Commissioner = Chief Executive of Municipal Corp
★PESA Act 1996 = Panchayats extended to 5th Schedule (tribal) areas
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st Municipal Corporation → Madras, 1687
➤Father of Local Self-Govt → Lord Ripon
➤1st to recommend 3-tier PR → Balwant Rai Mehta (1957)
➤1st state to implement PR → Rajasthan (Oct 2, 1959)
➤1st Co-operative law → Lord Curzon (1904)
➤PESA Act → 1996 (Bhuria Committee recommended)
➤97th CAA 2011 → Co-operative Societies (Part IXB)
➤PM at time of 73rd & 74th CAA → P.V. Narasimha Rao
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Which committee first recommended the 3-tier Panchayati Raj system?
Balwant Rai Mehta Committee (1957)
Q2
Which state first implemented Panchayati Raj and in which year?
Rajasthan — Oct 2, 1959 (Nagaur)
Q3
Which committee's report directly led to the 73rd CAA?
L.M. Singhvi Committee (1986)
Q4
The 73rd CAA came into force on which date?
24th April 1993 (Panchayati Raj Diwas)
Q5
Which Part and Articles were added by 73rd CAA?
Part IX — Articles 243 to 243O
Q6
Which Schedule was added by 73rd CAA and how many items?
11th Schedule — 29 items
Q7
How many tiers are in the Panchayati Raj system?
3 Tiers (Village, Block/Intermediate, District)
Q8
What is the minimum women's reservation in Panchayats?
Not less than 1/3rd (including Chairpersons)
Q9
What is the term of Panchayats?
5 years; if dissolved → elections within 6 months
Q10
What is the minimum age to become a Panchayat member?
21 Years
Q11
Who appoints the State Finance Commission for Panchayats?
Governor of the State
Q12
Who conducts elections to Panchayats?
State Election Commission (SEC)
Q13
Who appoints the State Election Commissioner?
Governor; removed like HC Judge
Q14
Which states are exempt from Part IX (Panchayati Raj)?
Nagaland, Meghalaya, Mizoram, Hill areas Manipur
Q15
What is the Gram Sabha according to Art. 243?
Body of persons registered in electoral rolls of a village
Q16
States with population ≤ how many don't need an intermediate Panchayat tier?
20 Lakh population
Q17
PESA Act 1996 extends Panchayat provisions to which areas?
Fifth Schedule (Scheduled Tribal) Areas
Q18
Which committee recommended PESA provisions?
Bhuria Committee
Q19
The 74th CAA came into force on which date?
1st June 1993
Q20
Which Part and Articles were added by 74th CAA?
Part IXA — Articles 243P to 243ZG
Q21
Which Schedule was added by 74th CAA and how many items?
12th Schedule — 18 items
Q22
Which was the first Municipal Corporation in India?
Madras (1687–88)
Q23
Who is called the Father of Local Self-Government in India?
Lord Ripon
Q24
Lord Ripon's Resolution 1882 is known as?
Magna Carta of Local Self-Government
Q25
The 65th CAA (Nagar Palika Bill) was rejected by which House?
Rajya Sabha (1989)
Q26
Which Article makes it obligatory for states to constitute Municipalities?
Article 243Q
Q27
What type of Urban Local Body is for transitional (rural-to-urban) areas?
Nagar Panchayat
Q28
Who heads a Municipal Corporation?
Mayor
Q29
Who is the chief executive authority of a Municipal Corporation?
Municipal Commissioner (appointed by State Govt)
Q30
Metropolitan Planning Committee is mandated for cities with population above?
10 Lakh (Art. 243ZE)
Q31
District Planning Committee is provided under which Article?
Article 243ZD
Q32
Which Constitutional Amendment gave status to Co-operative Societies?
97th CAA, 2011 (Part IXB)
Q33
Who passed the first co-operative law in India (1904)?
Lord Curzon
Q34
How many types of Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) are there in India?
8 Types (incl. Municipal Corp, Municipality, Nagar Panchayat, Cantonment Board etc.)
Q35
Cantonment Board is established under which Act?
Cantonments Act (1924)
Q36
Who is the President of a Cantonment Board?
Station Commander (Stn. Commander)
Q37
PM at the time of 73rd and 74th CAA was?
P.V. Narasimha Rao
Q38
Gram Sabha is described as the embodiment of which type of democracy?
Direct Democracy
Q39
Ashok Mehta Committee (1977) proposed which tier system?
2-tier (Zilla Parishad + Mandal Panchayat)
Q40
Can courts interfere in Panchayat constituency delimitation? (Art. 243O)
No — Courts CANNOT interfere
