Indian Polity – Chapter 11 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 11 of 14
Chapter 12 Constitutional Bodies — ECI · UPSC · CAG
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Constitutional &
Non-Constitutional Bodies
📖 Chapter 12
🏛️ ECI, UPSC, CAG, FC
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ ECI  ●  UPSC  ●  Finance Commission  ●  CAG  ●  Lokpal  ●  NHRC  ●  NITI Aayog  ●  40 MCQs
📂 PART A : THREE TYPES OF GOVERNMENT BODIES Classification
🏛️ Constitutional Bodies
Created by: The Constitution itself
Altered by: Constitutional Amendment (Art. 368)
Examples: ECI, UPSC, CAG, Finance Commission, GST Council, AG
📜 Statutory Bodies
Created by: An Act of Parliament (law)
Altered by: Amendment of the Act
Examples: NHRC, CIC, CVC, NCBC (pre-2018), Lokpal
⚙️ Non-Statutory / Executive Bodies
Created by: Executive Order / Govt. Resolution
Altered by: Executive order
Examples: CBI, NITI Aayog, Law Commission, NDC
🏛️ PART B : CONSTITUTIONAL BODIES — Key Details 6 Major Bodies
🗳️ Election Commission of India (ECI)
Arts. 324–329 | Part XV
  • Permanent & Independent Constitutional body
  • Appointment by President | Tenure: 6 years or 65 yrs
  • Salary: Same as SC Judge
  • CEC removal: Same as SC Judge | Other ECs: On CEC recommendation
  • Originally single-member; multi-member since 1989
  • ⭐ KEY Conducts: LS, RS, State LA, President, VP elections. NOT Panchayat/Municipality
  • 1st CEC: Sukumar Sen | 1st Female CEC: Rama Devi
  • 61st CAA 1988: Voting age reduced 21 → 18 years
  • EVM first used: Kerala (Paravoor) | VVPAT: Nagaland (Noksen), Sept 2013
  • NOTA introduced: 2013
📊 ECI — Article-wise + State EC
Arts. 324–329
  • Art. 324 — Superintendence, direction & control of elections
  • Art. 325 — No ineligibility on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex
  • Art. 326 — Elections on basis of Adult Suffrage
  • Art. 329 — Bar on court interference in electoral matters
State Election Commission: Appointed by Governor | Removed by President | Tenure: 6 yrs or 62 yrs | Salary: Same as HC Judge | Conducts Panchayat & Municipality elections
🏆 National Party Criteria: 2% LS seats from 3+ states OR 6% votes + 4 LS seats OR State party in 4+ states
📋 Union Public Service Commission (UPSC)
Arts. 315–323 | Part XIV
  • Known as 'Watchdog of Merit System'
  • Appointment by President | Tenure: 6 yrs or 65 yrs
  • At least half of members must have held govt office for 10 years
  • Removal by President: insolvency, office of profit, infirmity, misbehaviour (SC inquiry)
  • Cornwallis = Father of Civil Services | Patel = Father of Modern Civil Services
  • Charter Act 1853 → ICS | GoI Act 1919 → Federal PSC | Lee Commission 1924 → Federal PSC (1926)
  • 1st Chairman: Sir Ross Barker (pre-Independence) | H.K. Kripalani (post-Independence)
SPSC: Appointed by Governor | Removed by President | Tenure: 6 yrs or 62 yrs | Joint PSC = Statutory (not Constitutional)
💰 Finance Commission (Art. 280)
Art. 280 | Every 5 Years
  • Constituted every 5 years by President
  • Nature: Quasi-Judicial (powers of Civil Court)
  • Composition: Chairman + 4 other members
  • Chairman: must have experience in public affairs
  • Recommends sharing of divisible taxes — Centre & States
  • Recommendations: NOT binding on President
  • 1st FC (1951): K.C. Neogy | 14th FC: Y.V. Reddy | 15th FC: N.K. Singh
  • ⭐ KEY 16th FC (from Apr 1, 2025): Arvind Panagariya
🔍 Comptroller & Auditor General (CAG)
Arts. 148–151 | Part V
  • Known as 'Guardian of Public Purse'
  • Ambedkar called CAG 'most important officer under Constitution'
  • Appointment by President | Tenure: 6 yrs or 65 yrs
  • Single-member body | NOT eligible for re-appointment
  • Removal: Same as SC Judge
  • Audits: Contingency Fund, Consolidated Fund, Public Accounts (Centre & States)
  • Compiles State accounts (NOT Central Govt accounts)
  • Acts as Guide to Public Accounts Committee (PAC)
  • PAC: 15 LS members + 7 RS members
🏛️ Other Constitutional Bodies
GST Council + National Commissions
  • GST Council (Art. 279-A) — 101st CAA 2016 | Chairperson: Union Finance Minister | Joint forum of Centre & States
  • NC for SCs (Art. 338) — 89th CAA 2003 | 3-year tenure | Quasi-judicial
  • NC for STs (Art. 338-A) — 89th CAA 2003 | 3-year tenure
  • NC for BCs (Art. 338-B) — Constitutional status by 102nd CAA 2018
  • All 3 commissions: Chairperson + VC + 3 members | Appointed by President
  • Special Officer for Linguistic Minorities (Art. 350-B) — 7th CAA 1956 | Appointed by President
  • Advocate General (Art. 165) — Appointed by Governor; qualified as HC Judge; no fixed tenure
📜 NC History: Art. 338 originally = Special Officer for SCs & STs | 65th CAA 1990 = Multi-member NC for SCs & STs | 89th CAA 2003 = Bifurcated into NC for SCs (338) & NC for STs (338-A) | NCBC originally Statutory (1993, post-Mandal) → 102nd CAA 2018 gave Constitutional status (Art. 338-B)
⚙️ PART C : NON-CONSTITUTIONAL BODIES — Lokpal, NHRC, NITI Aayog Statutory + Executive
⚖️ Lokpal (Statutory)
  • Lokpal & Lokayukta Act, 2013 | Force: 16 Jan 2014
  • Ombudsman function — anti-corruption
  • 1 Chairperson + max 8 members
  • Term: 5 yrs or 70 yrs
  • Selection Committee: PM (Chair), Speaker LS, Leader of Opp, CJI, 1 jurist
  • 1st Chairperson: Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose
  • Lokayukta: State-level | 1st state: Maharashtra (Oct 25, 1972)
  • 1st state to pass Lokayukta Act: Odisha
  • Most powerful: Karnataka Lokayukta
  • Term 'Lokpal' coined by: Dr. L.M. Singhvi
  • First Ombudsman: Sweden 1809
👁️ NHRC (Statutory)
  • Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
  • Known as 'Watchdog of Human Rights'
  • Chairperson: Retired CJI or Retired SC Judge
  • Composition: Chair + 5 full-time + 4 ex-officio (NC Women, SCs, STs, Minorities)
  • Appointment: President | 6-member committee: PM, Speaker LS, Dy Chair RS, Opp Leaders, Home Minister
  • Tenure: 3 yrs or 70 yrs
  • NOT eligible for re-appointment in Central/State Govt
  • State HRC: Appointed by Governor | Removal by President | 3 yrs or 70 yrs
🗂️ CIC, CVC & CBI
  • CIC — RTI Act 2005 | CIC + 10 Info Commissioners | Appointed by President | 5 yrs or 65 yrs | NOT eligible for re-appointment
  • CIC appointment committee: PM, Leader of Opp, Home Minister
  • CVC — Est. 1964 (Executive); Statutory 2003 | Recommended by Santhanam Committee | CVC + 2 Vigilance Commissioners | Appointment: PM, Home Minister, Leader of Opp (LS)
  • CBI — Executive (Delhi Police Estb. Act 1946 for powers) | Est. 1963 | Appointment by 3-member committee: PM, Leader of Opp LS, CJI | 5-year tenure
🏛️ NITI Aayog — National Institution for Transforming India
Established
1 Jan 2015
Replaced
Planning Commission (1950)
Nature
Executive (Think Tank)
Approach
Cooperative Federalism
Composition:
  • • Chairperson: Prime Minister
  • • 1st VC: Arvind Panagariya | Present VC: Suman Bery
  • • 1st CEO: Sindhushree Kullar | Present: BVR Subrahmanyam
  • • Governing Council: CMs of all States + LGs of UTs
Law Commission:
  • • Nature: Executive — Neither Statutory NOR Constitutional
  • • 1st Law Commission: 1834 | Chair: T.B. Macaulay
  • • 1st post-Independence: 1955 | Chair: M.C. Setalvad
  • • Advisory to Ministry of Law & Justice (NOT binding)
📊 QUICK COMPARISON — All Major Bodies at a Glance ⭐ Must Know!
BODYNATUREAPPOINTMENTTENUREREMOVAL
ECI / CECConstitutionalPresident6 yrs or 65 yrsLike SC Judge
UPSC ChairmanConstitutionalPresident6 yrs or 65 yrsPresident (SC inquiry)
Finance CommissionConstitutionalPresident (every 5 yrs)As determined—
CAGConstitutionalPresident6 yrs or 65 yrsLike SC Judge | No re-appointment
Attorney GeneralConstitutionalPresidentPleasure of President—
State EC CommissionerConstitutionalGovernor6 yrs or 62 yrsPresident (like HC Judge)
SPSC ChairmanConstitutionalGovernor6 yrs or 62 yrsPresident
LokpalStatutoryPresident (Select. Comm.)5 yrs or 70 yrsPresident
NHRC ChairpersonStatutoryPresident (6-member comm.)3 yrs or 70 yrsPresident | No re-appt
CICStatutoryPresident (PM, LO, HM)5 yrs or 65 yrsPresident | No re-appt
CVCStatutoryPresident (PM, HM, LO-LS)4 yrs or 65 yrsPresident
CBI DirectorExecutive3-member comm. (PM, LO, CJI)5 years—
NITI AayogExecutivePM (Chairperson)——
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗ECI conducts Pres/VP/LS/RS/LA elections
NOT Panchayat/Municipality (those = SEC)
✗UPSC appointed by President
SPSC appointed by Governor but removed by President
✗Joint PSC = Statutory (not Constitutional)
UPSC + SPSC = Constitutional
✗CAG = NOT eligible for re-appointment
Finance Commission members = eligible for re-appointment
✗NCBC = Statutory till 2018 → Constitutional (102nd CAA 2018)
✗First Ombudsman → Sweden 1809
First in India → Lokpal Act 2013
✅ RAPID RECALL
★CAG = Guardian of Public Purse | Ambedkar = "Most important officer"
★1st CEC = Sukumar Sen | 1st Female CEC = Rama Devi
★Voting age reduced 21→18 = 61st CAA 1988
★NOTA introduced = 2013
★16th FC Chair = Arvind Panagariya (from Apr 1, 2025)
★1st Lokpal Chairperson = Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose
★NITI Aayog replaced Planning Commission → 1 Jan 2015
★CBI appointment = PM + Leader of Opp + CJI
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤1st CEC → Sukumar Sen
➤1st state to pass Lokayukta Act → Odisha
➤1st Lokpal Chairperson → Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose
➤1st Ombudsman globally → Sweden, 1809
➤1st EVM use → Kerala (Paravoor constituency)
➤1st VVPAT use → Nagaland (Noksen), Sept 2013
➤Father of Civil Services → Lord Cornwallis
➤1st post-Independence Law Commission → 1955 (M.C. Setalvad)
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Election Commission of India is governed by which Articles?
Articles 324–329 (Part XV)
Q2
Who appoints the Chief Election Commissioner?
President of India
Q3
What is the tenure of the Chief Election Commissioner?
6 years or 65 years (whichever earlier)
Q4
How is the CEC removed from office?
Same procedure as SC Judge (impeachment)
Q5
Who was the first Chief Election Commissioner of India?
Sukumar Sen
Q6
Which constitutional amendment reduced voting age from 21 to 18?
61st CAA, 1988
Q7
Where was EVM first used in India?
Kerala — Paravoor Assembly Constituency
Q8
When and where was VVPAT first used?
September 2013 — Nagaland (Noksen constituency)
Q9
ECI does NOT conduct elections to which bodies?
Panchayats and Municipalities (those = State EC)
Q10
State Election Commissioner is appointed by whom and removed by whom?
Appointed by Governor; Removed by President
Q11
UPSC is known as what?
Watchdog of Merit System
Q12
What is the tenure of UPSC members?
6 years or 65 years (whichever earlier)
Q13
Who is known as the Father of Civil Services in India?
Lord Cornwallis
Q14
Who is known as Father of Indian Modern Civil Services?
Sardar Vallabhbhai Patel
Q15
Joint PSC is what type of body — constitutional or statutory?
Statutory (not Constitutional)
Q16
Finance Commission is constituted by whom and how often?
President — Every 5 Years (Art. 280)
Q17
Who was the Chairman of the 1st Finance Commission (1951)?
K.C. Neogy
Q18
Who heads the 16th Finance Commission (from April 2025)?
Arvind Panagariya
Q19
Are Finance Commission recommendations binding on the President?
No — NOT binding
Q20
Who called CAG the "most important officer under the Constitution"?
Dr. B.R. Ambedkar
Q21
What is the tenure of the CAG?
6 years or 65 years (No re-appointment)
Q22
CAG removal is same as removal of whom?
Supreme Court Judge
Q23
GST Council was created by which Amendment?
101st CAA, 2016 (Art. 279-A)
Q24
Who chairs the GST Council?
Union Finance Minister
Q25
NCBC got Constitutional status by which Amendment?
102nd CAA, 2018 (Art. 338-B)
Q26
Term 'Lokpal' and 'Lokayukta' was coined by whom?
Dr. L.M. Singhvi
Q27
Lokpal & Lokayukta Act came into force on which date?
16th January 2014
Q28
What is the tenure of Lokpal members?
5 years or 70 years (whichever earlier)
Q29
Who was the first Lokpal Chairperson of India?
Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose
Q30
Which was the first state where Lokayukta came into effect?
Maharashtra (25th October 1972)
Q31
Which state was first to pass Lokayukta Act?
Odisha
Q32
NHRC was established under which Act?
Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
Q33
What is the tenure of NHRC Chairperson?
3 years or 70 years (No re-appointment)
Q34
CIC was established under which Act and in which year?
Right to Information Act, 2005
Q35
CVC was recommended by which committee?
Santhanam Committee
Q36
Who is included in the 3-member committee for CBI Director appointment?
PM, Leader of Opposition LS, CJI
Q37
NITI Aayog replaced which body and in which year?
Planning Commission — 1st January 2015
Q38
Who was the 1st Vice-Chairperson of NITI Aayog?
Arvind Panagariya
Q39
First Law Commission of India was formed in which year and who chaired it?
1834 — T.B. Macaulay (under Charter Act 1833)
Q40
Which country first established the institution of Ombudsman?
Sweden (1809)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 12 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 12 of 14
Chapter 13 Amendments — Art. 368 — 106 CAAs
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Amendments of the Constitution
Article 368 — Part XX
📖 Chapter 13
⚖️ 106 Amendments (till 2026)
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ 3 Types of Amendments  ●  Art. 368 Procedure  ●  Key CAAs  ●  42nd vs 44th  ●  8th Schedule Languages  ●  40 MCQs
📜 PART A : INTRODUCTION — India vs Britain vs USA Art. 368 | Part XX
🌍 India vs Britain vs USA
FeatureIndiaBritainUSA
ProcedureMix — 3 typesSimple MajorityVery Rigid
NatureNeither rigid nor flexibleFlexibleRigid
Key ArticleArt. 368, Part XXNo written constitutionArticle V
SourceSouth African Constitution——
📌 Key Facts about Art. 368
  • Amendment by way of addition, variation or repeal of any provision
  • Parliament exercises its constituent power (not ordinary legislative power)
  • ⭐ KEY Parliament CANNOT amend Basic Structure — Kesavananda Bharati 1973
  • Total Amendments as of April 2026: 106 Constitutional Amendment Acts
  • Amendment procedure borrowed from South African Constitution
📂 PART B : THREE TYPES OF CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENTS ⭐ Must Know!
A — Simple Majority
Outside Art. 368
More than 50% of members present & voting
State ratification: NOT required
  • Formation/alteration of States
  • Creation/abolition of Legislative Councils
  • 2nd Schedule (salaries of President, Governors etc.)
  • Quorum in Parliament | Salaries of MPs
  • Rules of procedure in Parliament
  • Citizenship | Official language | Union Territories
  • 5th & 6th Schedules
  • President's Rule (Art. 356) approval
  • Financial Emergency (Art. 360) approval
B — Special Majority
Under Art. 368
Majority of total membership of each House + 2/3rd of members present & voting
State ratification: NOT required
  • Fundamental Rights (Part III)
  • Directive Principles of State Policy (Part IV)
  • All other provisions not in Type A or Type C
💡 Total Membership: Total members comprising the House irrespective of vacancies or absentees
C — Special Majority
+ State Ratification
Special majority + Ratification by at least ½ of State Legislatures (by simple majority each)
No time limit for states to give consent
  • Election of President (Arts. 54, 55)
  • Extent of executive power of Union & States
  • Supreme Court and High Courts
  • Distribution of legislative powers (Union & States)
  • GST Council (Art. 279-A)
  • 7th Schedule (Union, State, Concurrent Lists)
  • Representation of States in Parliament
  • Article 368 itself
⚙️ PART C : AMENDMENT PROCEDURE (Art. 368) — Step by Step 8 Steps
1
IntroductionBill can be introduced in EITHER House of Parliament (LS or RS)
2
Who Can IntroduceAny Minister OR Private Member — President's prior permission is NOT required
3
Passage in Each HouseMust be passed by SPECIAL MAJORITY in each House separately
4
No Joint SittingIf disagreement between Houses — NO provision for Joint Sitting (unlike ordinary bills)
5
State Ratification (if applicable)For federal provisions: ratified by at least ½ state legislatures by simple majority | No time limit
6
Presidential AssentBill presented to President after passage by both Houses
7
President's ObligationPresident MUST give assent — CANNOT withhold or return the Bill (24th CAA made this compulsory)
8
EnactmentAfter Presidential assent → becomes a Constitutional Amendment Act
📋 PART D : ALL IMPORTANT CONSTITUTIONAL AMENDMENT ACTS 1st to 106th CAA
CAA & YEARKEY CHANGES / HIGHLIGHTS
1st CAA
1951
State empowered to make special provisions for backward classes | 9th Schedule added (protect land reform laws from judicial review) | Additional restrictions on Art. 19
7th CAA
1956
Common HC for 2+ states | Union Territories created | Removed Part-VII | Art. 350-B — Commissioner for Linguistic Minorities
24th CAA
1971
Presidential assent to CAA made compulsory | Parliament affirmed power to amend FRs
26th CAA
1971
Privy Purse and privileges of former rulers abolished
42nd CAA
1976
⭐ MINI CONSTITUTION Added Socialist, Secular, Integrity to Preamble | 5 subjects State→Concurrent List | Added FDs (Part IVA, Art. 51A) | Added Tribunals (Part XIVA) | Extended LS term to 6 yrs | Curtailed judicial review
44th CAA
1978
Armed Rebellion replaced 'Internal Disturbance' | Art. 31 removed from FRs (→ Art. 300A) | Arts. 20 & 21 CANNOT be suspended during NE | Cabinet written recommendation mandatory for NE | Restored 5-year LS term
52nd CAA
1985
10th Schedule added — Anti-Defection Law (applies to MPs and MLAs)
61st CAA
1989
Voting age reduced 21 → 18 years for LS and State Assembly elections
69th CAA
1991
Delhi given special status — National Capital Territory (NCT)
73rd CAA
1992
Panchayati Raj given Constitutional status | 11th Schedule added (29 subjects)
74th CAA
1992
Municipalities given Constitutional status | 12th Schedule added (18 subjects)
86th CAA
2002
Art. 21-A added — Free & compulsory education (6–14 yrs) made FR | Art. 51-A(k) added — duty of parents to provide education
89th CAA
2003
NC for SCs & STs bifurcated — NCSC (Art. 338) and NCST (Art. 338-A)
91st CAA
2003
Council of Ministers limited to 15% of LS/State Assembly strength
92nd CAA
2003
Maithili, Santhali, Dogri, Bodo added to 8th Schedule → total 22 languages
97th CAA
2011
Co-operative Societies given Constitutional status | Right to form co-operatives = FR (Art. 19) | Art. 43-B added as DPSP | New Part IX-B added
99th CAA
2014
Established NJAC — declared unconstitutional by SC in 2015 (4th Judges Case)
101st CAA
2016
GST introduced | Arts. 246-A, 269-A, 279-A inserted | GST Council established
102nd CAA
2018
Constitutional status to NCBC (Art. 338-B)
103rd CAA
2019
10% EWS reservation added to Arts. 15 & 16 (Economically Weaker Sections)
104th CAA
2020
Extended SC/ST reservation in LS/State Assemblies from 70 to 80 years | Removed Anglo-Indian reserved seats
106th CAA
2023
Women's Reservation — reserved 1/3rd seats for women in LS, State Assemblies & Delhi Assembly
⚡ PART E : 42nd CAA vs 44th CAA — Key Comparison ⭐ Most Asked!
42nd CAA, 1976 — "Mini Constitution" 🚨
Enacted during Emergency | PM: Indira Gandhi | Swaran Singh Committee
  • Added Socialist, Secular, Integrity to Preamble
  • 5 subjects transferred from State → Concurrent List (Education, Forests, Weights & Measures, Wild Animals, Justice)
  • Added Fundamental Duties (Part IVA, Art. 51-A)
  • Added Tribunals (Part XIVA — Arts. 323A, 323B)
  • Expanded DPSPs — Arts. 39, 39A, 43A, 48A
  • Extended LS & State Assembly term to 6 years
  • Curtailed power of courts to review CAAs
  • Gave precedence to DPSPs over FRs (Art. 31C amended)
44th CAA, 1978 — Janata Govt's Response ✅
Enacted by Janata Govt to undo controversial 42nd CAA changes
  • Changed Internal Disturbance → Armed Rebellion (higher threshold for NE)
  • Written Cabinet recommendation mandatory for President to proclaim NE
  • Art. 31 (Right to Property) removed from FRs → made legal right under Art. 300-A
  • ⭐ KEY Arts. 20 & 21 CANNOT be suspended during National Emergency
  • Art. 38: State to minimise inequalities in income, status, facilities
  • Restored 5-year term of LS & State Assemblies
  • Restored power of courts to review CAAs (partially)
🗣️ PART F : 8th SCHEDULE — Language Additions Now 22 Languages
CAA & YEARLANGUAGES ADDEDTOTAL
Original 1950Hindi, Bengali, Gujarati, Kannada, Kashmiri, Malayalam, Marathi, Oriya, Punjabi, Sanskrit, Tamil, Telugu, Urdu, Assamese14
21st CAA, 1967Sindhi15
71st CAA, 1992Konkani, Manipuri, Nepali18
92nd CAA, 2003Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali (Mnemonic: MSD the Big)22 (Current)
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗Simple Majority amendments are OUTSIDE Art. 368
Special Majority = UNDER Art. 368
✗No Joint Sitting for CAA Bills
Joint Sitting only for ordinary bills
✗President CANNOT withhold assent to CAA Bill
Made compulsory by 24th CAA 1971
✗42nd CAA = Added 'Socialist, Secular, Integrity'
NOT in original Preamble
✗42nd CAA: LS term → 6 years
44th CAA: Restored to 5 years
✗8th Schedule: 22 languages (not 14 or 18)
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Amendment procedure borrowed from South Africa
★Total amendments: 106 (as of April 2026)
★Basic Structure cannot be amended → Kesavananda Bharati 1973
★9th Schedule added → 1st CAA 1951
★Anti-Defection Law → 10th Schedule, 52nd CAA 1985
★42nd CAA = "Mini Constitution" | Recommended by Swaran Singh Committee
★Women's Reservation = 1/3rd seats, 106th CAA 2023
★EWS 10% Reservation → 103rd CAA 2019
🥇 FIRST-EVER / KEY FACTS
➤1st CAA in India → 1951 (9th Schedule added)
➤Most comprehensive amendment → 42nd CAA 1976 (Mini Constitution)
➤Privy Purse abolished → 26th CAA 1971
➤Sikkim became 22nd state → 36th CAA 1975
➤Voting age reduced → 61st CAA 1989
➤GST introduced → 101st CAA 2016
➤4 languages added to 8th Schedule in 2003 → Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali
➤NJAC struck down → 99th CAA 2014 (4th Judges Case 2015)
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Which Article deals with the power to amend the Constitution?
Article 368 (Part XX)
Q2
India's amendment procedure was borrowed from which country?
South African Constitution
Q3
How many Constitutional Amendments have been made till April 2026?
106 Constitutional Amendment Acts
Q4
Parliament CANNOT amend what, as ruled in Kesavananda Bharati 1973?
Basic Structure of the Constitution
Q5
Simple majority amendments are under which Article?
OUTSIDE Article 368 (ordinary legislative process)
Q6
Special majority means majority of total membership + what?
2/3rd of members present and voting
Q7
Which type of amendment requires State Ratification?
Special Majority + Ratification by at least ½ of States
Q8
Is there a time limit for States to ratify a Constitutional Amendment?
No — No time limit
Q9
Can a Constitutional Amendment Bill be introduced in Rajya Sabha?
Yes — Either House (LS or RS)
Q10
Does the President need to approve before introducing a CAA Bill?
No — President's prior permission NOT required
Q11
Is there a provision for Joint Sitting for Constitutional Amendment Bills?
No — NO Joint Sitting for CAA Bills
Q12
Can the President withhold assent to a Constitutional Amendment Bill?
No — Must give assent (24th CAA made it compulsory)
Q13
Which CAA added the 9th Schedule to protect land reform laws?
1st CAA, 1951
Q14
Which CAA made Presidential assent to CAA Bill compulsory?
24th CAA, 1971
Q15
Which CAA abolished Privy Purse and privileges of former rulers?
26th CAA, 1971
Q16
Which CAA is called the "Mini Constitution" of India?
42nd CAA, 1976
Q17
Which words were added to the Preamble by 42nd CAA?
Socialist, Secular, Integrity
Q18
42nd CAA was based on recommendations of which committee?
Swaran Singh Committee
Q19
How many subjects were transferred from State to Concurrent List by 42nd CAA?
5 subjects (Education, Forests, Weights & Measures, Wild Animals, Justice)
Q20
Which CAA added Fundamental Duties (Part IVA) to the Constitution?
42nd CAA, 1976
Q21
Which CAA changed 'Internal Disturbance' to 'Armed Rebellion'?
44th CAA, 1978
Q22
Which two Articles cannot be suspended even during National Emergency?
Articles 20 and 21 (44th CAA, 1978)
Q23
Which CAA removed the Right to Property from Fundamental Rights?
44th CAA, 1978 (moved to Art. 300-A)
Q24
Which CAA added the Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule)?
52nd CAA, 1985
Q25
Which CAA reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years?
61st CAA, 1989
Q26
Which CAA gave Delhi the status of National Capital Territory?
69th CAA, 1991
Q27
Which CAA gave Constitutional status to Panchayati Raj + added 11th Schedule?
73rd CAA, 1992
Q28
Which CAA added the 12th Schedule for Municipalities?
74th CAA, 1992
Q29
Which CAA made free education (6–14 years) a Fundamental Right?
86th CAA, 2002 (Art. 21-A)
Q30
Which CAA bifurcated NC for SCs & STs into two separate bodies?
89th CAA, 2003
Q31
Which CAA limited Council of Ministers to 15% of LS strength?
91st CAA, 2003
Q32
Which CAA added Bodo, Dogri, Maithili, Santhali to 8th Schedule?
92nd CAA, 2003
Q33
Which CAA gave Constitutional status to Co-operative Societies?
97th CAA, 2011
Q34
Which CAA established NJAC (later struck down)?
99th CAA, 2014
Q35
Which CAA introduced GST in India?
101st CAA, 2016
Q36
Which CAA gave Constitutional status to NCBC (Art. 338-B)?
102nd CAA, 2018
Q37
Which CAA introduced 10% EWS reservation?
103rd CAA, 2019
Q38
Which CAA removed Anglo-Indian reserved seats from Parliament?
104th CAA, 2020
Q39
How many languages are currently in the 8th Schedule?
22 Languages
Q40
Which CAA reserved 1/3rd seats for women in LS & State Assemblies?
106th CAA, 2023 (Women's Reservation Act)
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 13 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 13 of 14
Chapter 14 Centre–State Relations — Parts XI & XII
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
Centre–State Relations
Legislative, Administrative & Financial
📖 Chapter 14
⚖️ Part XI & XII — Arts. 245–293
⚡ Final Revision
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
⭐ Exam Ready ⭐
⚡ 7th Schedule  ●  5 Conditions  ●  Financial Relations  ●  Sarkaria Commission  ●  Inter-State Council  ●  40 MCQs
🗺️ PART A : THREE DOMAINS OF CENTRE–STATE RELATIONS Parts XI & XII
📜 Legislative Relations
Arts. 245–255 | Part XI

Territorial extent of laws, 7th Schedule (Union, State, Concurrent Lists), Parliament's power on State List, Residuary powers
⚙️ Administrative Relations
Arts. 256–263 | Part XI

Distribution of executive power, All India Services, Centre's directions to States, Inter-State Council
💰 Financial Relations
Arts. 264–293 | Part XII

Taxing powers, distribution of revenues, grants-in-aid, borrowing powers
📋 PART B : 7th SCHEDULE — 3 LISTS (Legislative Distribution) ⭐ Most Asked!
LISTART.SUBJECTSWHO LEGISLATESKEY EXAMPLES
Union List
 246 ~98 subjects Only Parliament Defence, Banking, Atomic Energy, Foreign Affairs, Railways, Currency, Post & Telegraph, Census, Customs, Income Tax
State List
 246 ~61 subjects Only State Legislature Public Order, Police, Prisons, Agriculture, Fisheries, Markets, Land, Local Government, Excise on Liquor
Concurrent List
 246 ~52 subjects Both Parliament & State
Centre prevails in conflict		 Education, Forests, Trade Unions, Marriage, Adoption, Criminal Law, Bankruptcy, Population Control, Civil Procedure
Residuary Subjects
 248 NOT in any list Only Parliament Cybercrimes, Space, Internet (modern topics)
Unlike USA — India's residual powers vest with CENTRE
⚠️ Conflict Resolution (Art. 254): Centre vs State law on Concurrent List → Centre prevails. BUT if State law received Presidential assent → it overrides Central law in that state  |  Surcharges on taxes → 100% retained by Centre (not shared)
⚡ PART C : 5 CONDITIONS — Parliament Legislates on State List Arts. 249–356
#CONDITIONARTICLEKEY PROVISIONS
A
National Interest — RS Resolution
249
  • RS passes resolution by 2/3rd of members present & voting → Parliament can legislate on State List
  • Valid for 1 year (renewable) | State legislature can ALSO make laws on same subject
B
National Emergency
250
  • During National Emergency: Parliament gets FULL power to legislate on State List
  • State laws can also be made; Central law prevails. Centre laws cease 6 months after emergency ends
C
Request by States
252
  • 2 or more State Legislatures pass resolutions requesting Parliament to legislate
  • Law applies only to requesting states | Can be amended/repealed ONLY by Parliament (not State)
  • Examples: Wildlife Protection Act 1972, Water (Prevention & Control of Pollution) Act 1974
D
International Agreements
253
  • Parliament can legislate on any State List subject to implement international treaties, agreements or conventions
  • Examples: Geneva Convention Act 1960, TRIPS-related legislation, Environment laws
E
President's Rule
356
  • During President's Rule: Parliament can make laws on State List for that state
  • Even AFTER President's Rule ends, such laws continue — but State legislature can repeal/alter/re-enact
⚙️ PART D : ADMINISTRATIVE RELATIONS — Arts. 256–263 Centre's Directions
📌 Centre's Directions to States (Art. 257)
  • Art. 257(1): States must NOT obstruct Central executive power
  • Art. 257(2): States must maintain communication links of national & military importance
  • Art. 257(3): States must ensure protection of Railways
  • State failure to comply → basis for imposing President's Rule (Art. 365)
🏛️ All India Services (Art. 312)
  • Parliament creates new AIS via RS resolution
  • Existing AIS: IAS, IPS, IFS
  • Ultimate control: Central Govt | Day-to-day: State Govt
  • Recruited by UPSC | Service rules by Central Govt
  • Key instrument for maintaining all-India unity
SPSC: Appointed by Governor | Removed by President (not Governor)
💰 PART E : FINANCIAL RELATIONS — Taxing Powers & Revenue Distribution Arts. 264–293
⚡ Allocation of Taxing Powers
AUTHORITYTAXING POWERKEY EXAMPLES
Only Parliament
(Union List — 13 taxes)		Exclusively levied, collected & kept by CentreIncome Tax (non-agricultural), Corporation Tax, Customs Duty, Central Excise, Estate Duty, Stamp Duty on Bills of Exchange
Only States
(State List — 18 taxes)		Exclusively levied, collected & kept by StatesSales Tax/VAT, Excise on Liquor & Narcotics, Stamp Duty on Land, Octroi, Professional Tax, Entertainment Tax, Agricultural Income Tax
Both (after 101st CAA 2016)Parliament & State Legislature BOTH can make GST lawsCGST (Centre), SGST (State), IGST (shared)
Residuary (Art. 248)Parliament alone legislatesGift Tax, Wealth Tax, Expenditure Tax
📊 Distribution of Tax Revenues — Key Articles
ART.LEVYCOLLECTIONAPPROPRIATION/SHARINGKEY TAXES
268CentreStatesStates fullyStamp duties on shares, cheques, promissory notes, insurance policies
269CentreCentreStates fully — NOT part of CFITaxes on interstate trade & commerce
269-ACentre/StateCentreShared per GST Council formulaIGST — GST on interstate supplies (101st CAA 2016)
270CentreCentreShared Centre & States (per FC formula)Income Tax, Corporate Tax, all Union List taxes (except Arts. 268, 269, 271)
271CentreCentreCentre ONLY — not sharedSurcharge on taxes under Arts. 268, 269, 270 — 100% retained by Centre
💵 Grants-in-Aid to States
  • Art. 275 — Statutory Grants: Parliament makes grants to states in need. Obligatory. Example: SC/ST welfare grants
  • Art. 282 — Discretionary Grants: Both Centre AND States can make grants for any public purpose. Non-obligatory
  • Finance Commission recommends distribution under Art. 275
🏦 Borrowing Powers
  • Centre: Can borrow within India OR outside India, up to limit fixed by Parliament
  • States: Can borrow ONLY within India
  • State CANNOT borrow from outside India without Central Govt consent
  • If state has outstanding loan from Centre → Centre can impose conditions before fresh loans
📊 PART F : KEY COMMISSIONS ON CENTRE-STATE RELATIONS 4 Commissions
1966

ARC-I (Morarji Desai)

  • 1st Administrative Reforms Commission
  • Recommended establishment of Inter-State Council under Art. 263
1969

Rajamannar Committee (Tamil Nadu)

  • Head: P.V. Rajamannar | To review Centre-State relations & grant more autonomy to states
  • FC should be made a PERMANENT body
  • Arts. 356, 357, 365 (President's Rule) should be TOTALLY OMITTED
  • All India Services (IAS, IPS, IFS) should be ABOLISHED
1983

Sarkaria Commission (R.S. Sarkaria)

  • To examine working of existing Centre-State arrangements
  • Centre should consult states BEFORE making laws on Concurrent List subjects
  • Procedure for consulting CM in Governor appointment should be written into Constitution
  • Governor's appointment: must consult CM of that state
  • Led to establishment of Inter-State Council (1990)
2007

Punchhi Commission (M.M. Punchhi)

  • Post-Sarkaria review of Centre-State relations
  • Greater flexibility to states on State & Concurrent List subjects
  • Governor should be a person from OUTSIDE the state
  • Governors should have FIXED 5-YEAR TENURE and removal NOT at Centre's discretion
🏛️ Inter-State Council (Article 263)
Established
1990
Recommended by
Sarkaria Comm. + ARC-I
Chairperson
Prime Minister
Members
CMs of all States + LGs/Admins of UTs + 6 Cabinet Ministers
Functions: Inquire into disputes between states | Advise on matters of common interest | Recommend coordinated policy on common subjects
⚠️ DON'T CONFUSE!
✗Residual powers → Centre in India
In USA → Residual powers go to States
✗Art. 249 RS resolution → valid for 1 year (not permanent)
Can be renewed annually
✗Art. 252 (State Request): Law applies only to requesting states
Can only be amended/repealed by Parliament
✗Art. 271 Surcharges → 100% retained by Centre (NOT shared with states)
✗Art. 275 = Statutory (Obligatory) grants
Art. 282 = Discretionary (Non-obligatory) grants
✗States can borrow only within India
Centre can borrow from outside India too
✅ RAPID RECALL
★Union List ≈ 98 | State List ≈ 61 | Concurrent ≈ 52 subjects
★Centre prevails when conflict on Concurrent List (Art. 254)
★5 Conditions for Parliament on State List: A-B-C-D-E
★RS Resolution (Art. 249): 2/3rd majority → 1 year validity
★IAS, IPS, IFS = All India Services (Art. 312)
★Inter-State Council est. 1990 | Chaired by PM
★Sarkaria Commission (1983) → Led to Inter-State Council
★IGST on interstate supplies → Art. 269-A (101st CAA 2016)
🥇 FIRST-EVER FACTS
➤ARC-I head → Morarji Desai (1966)
➤Sarkaria Commission head → R.S. Sarkaria (1983)
➤Punchhi Commission head → M.M. Punchhi (2007)
➤ARC-II head → Veerappa Moily (2005)
➤Inter-State Council established → 1990
➤Rajamannar Committee (1969) = Tamil Nadu's demand for more autonomy
➤Wildlife Protection Act 1972 = example of Art. 252 (State request)
➤Geneva Convention Act 1960 = example of Art. 253 (International treaty)
🎯 MCQ-READY ONE-LINERS — Most Asked in SSC / RRB / Bank 40 Q&A ⚡
NO.
QUESTION
ANSWER
Q1
Centre-State legislative relations are covered under which Articles?
Articles 245–255 (Part XI)
Q2
How many subjects are in the Union List approximately?
~98 subjects
Q3
How many subjects are in the State List approximately?
~61 subjects
Q4
How many subjects are in the Concurrent List approximately?
~52 subjects
Q5
In which Article are Residuary Powers given to Parliament?
Article 248
Q6
In India, residual powers vest with whom? (unlike USA)
Parliament (Centre) — NOT States
Q7
If Centre & State laws conflict on Concurrent List — who prevails?
Centre (Art. 254)
Q8
Which Article allows Rajya Sabha to enable Parliament to legislate on State List?
Article 249
Q9
What majority is needed in RS under Art. 249?
2/3rd of members present and voting
Q10
How long is the Art. 249 resolution valid?
1 Year (renewable)
Q11
During National Emergency, Parliament can legislate on which list?
State List (Art. 250)
Q12
Centre laws on State List during NE cease how long after emergency ends?
6 Months after emergency ends
Q13
Which Article allows Parliament to legislate on State List at State's request?
Article 252
Q14
Under Art. 252, how many states must request Parliament to legislate?
Two or more State Legislatures
Q15
Wildlife Protection Act 1972 is an example of which Article?
Article 252 (State Request)
Q16
Which Article allows Parliament to legislate on State List for international treaties?
Article 253
Q17
Geneva Convention Act 1960 is an example of which Article?
Article 253
Q18
During President's Rule, Parliament can legislate on State List under which Article?
Article 356
Q19
Which Article says States must not obstruct Central executive power?
Article 257(1)
Q20
Which three services constitute All India Services?
IAS, IPS, IFS
Q21
Who has ultimate control over All India Services?
Central Government
Q22
State PSC members are appointed by Governor but removed by whom?
President of India
Q23
How is GST revenue from interstate supplies distributed? (which Article)
Article 269-A (101st CAA 2016)
Q24
Surcharges on taxes (Art. 271) are retained by whom?
Centre only — 100% (NOT shared)
Q25
Which Article provides for Statutory (obligatory) grants to States?
Article 275
Q26
Which Article provides for Discretionary (non-obligatory) grants?
Article 282
Q27
Can States borrow from outside India without Central permission?
No — Cannot borrow from outside India
Q28
Who headed the Sarkaria Commission and in which year was it set up?
R.S. Sarkaria — 1983
Q29
Sarkaria Commission's recommendation led to creation of which body?
Inter-State Council (1990)
Q30
Who chaired the Punchhi Commission and in which year?
M.M. Punchhi — 2007
Q31
Rajamannar Committee recommended AIS (IAS, IPS, IFS) should be what?
Abolished
Q32
Which Article provides for Inter-State Council?
Article 263
Q33
Who chairs the Inter-State Council?
Prime Minister
Q34
When was the Inter-State Council established?
1990
Q35
Under Art. 268, stamp duties are levied by Centre but collected by whom?
States (and fully retained by States)
Q36
Under Art. 270, revenues are shared between Centre and States — on whose recommendation?
Finance Commission
Q37
Which ARC recommended the Inter-State Council?
ARC-I (Morarji Desai, 1966)
Q38
Education was transferred from State List to Concurrent List by which CAA?
42nd CAA, 1976
Q39
State failure to comply with Centre's directions can lead to what?
President's Rule (Art. 356 via Art. 365)
Q40
GST was introduced by which Constitutional Amendment?
101st CAA, 2016
🏔 EVEREST COACHING POINT · HYDERABAD
Indian Polity – Chapter 14 | Final Revision
SSC · RRB · BANK ⭐
Chapter 14 of 14
📋 Final Overview — All 14 Chapters at a Glance
🏔 Everest Coaching Point · Hyderabad
INDIAN POLITY
Final Overview — All 14 Chapters
📖 Complete Revision
⚡ Last-Minute Prep
🎯 SSC · RRB · BANK
Target Exams
SSC · RRB · BANK
14 Chapters • Quick View
⚡ Historical Background  ●  Constitution  ●  FRs  ●  DPSP  ●  Parliament  ●  Judiciary  ●  States  ●  Local Bodies  ●  Amendments  ●  Centre-State

🔑 MASTER QUICK REFERENCE — Key Numbers & Facts

Constitution Adopted
26 Nov 1949
Republic Day (Enforced)
26 Jan 1950
Total Amendments
106 CAAs (till 2026)
Original Articles
395 | Now 470+
Original Schedules
8 | Now 12
Parts in Constitution
22 → 25 (current)
Fundamental Rights
Originally 7 → Now 6
Fundamental Duties
11 (Art. 51A, a–k)
LS Strength
Max 550 | Current 543
RS Strength
Max 250 | Current 245
SC Max Strength
34 (1 CJI + 33)
High Courts
25 in India
Union Territories
8 UTs
Panchayati Raj Day
24 April (73rd CAA)
8th Schedule Languages
22 Languages
Union List Subjects
~98 | State ~61 | Concurrent ~52
01
Historical Background
Company Rule (1773–1857) & Crown Rule (1858–1947)
1773 Regulating Act → 1st GG of Bengal: Warren Hastings
1784 Pitt's India Act → Dual Control (Half-Loaf System)
1833 Charter Act → GGI created; Slavery abolished; 1st GGI: Lord Bentinck
1858 GoI Act → EIC abolished; 1st Viceroy: Lord Canning
1909 IC Act → Direct elections; Father of Communal Electorate: Minto
1919 GoI Act → Dyarchy in provinces; 1st Bicameralism at Centre
⭐ 1935 GoI Act → 3 Lists; RBI; Provincial Autonomy — Blueprint of Constitution
1947 Independence Act → 1st & Last Indian GG: C. Rajagopalachari
02
Making of Constitution
Constituent Assembly 1946–1950
CA first proposed by M.N. Roy (1934); INC demanded it in 1935
CA constituted under Cabinet Mission Plan 1946; first met 9 Dec 1946
CA Chair: Dr. Rajendra Prasad | Drafting Committee: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar
Duration: 2 yrs, 11 months, 18 days | 11 sessions | 165 days | ₹64 lakhs
Objective Resolution: introduced 13 Dec 1946; adopted 22 Jan 1947
Constitution adopted: 26 Nov 1949 | Enforced: 26 Jan 1950
Sources: FRs from USA | DPSP from Ireland | FDs from USSR
⭐ 284 members signed | 15 women | English calligrapher: Prem Behari Narain Raizada
03
Preamble & Composition
SSDDR + Basic Structure
Preamble = "ID Card" (Palkhivala) | "Political Horoscope" (Munshi)
⭐ SSDDR: Sovereign + Socialist + Secular (42nd CAA 1976) + Democratic + Republic
4 Objectives: Justice (3 types) | Liberty (5 types) | Equality (2 types) | Fraternity
Berubari (1960): Preamble NOT part of Constitution
Kesavananda (1973): Preamble IS part + reflects Basic Structure
Basic Structure: Parliament CANNOT amend (Kesavananda Bharati, 1973)
India = Quasi-Federal (K.C. Wheare) | Cooperative Federalism (Granville Austin)
Current: 25 Parts | 470+ Articles | 12 Schedules
04
Territories & Citizenship
Arts. 1–11 + CAA 2019 + OCI/NRI
⭐ India = Union of States (NOT Federation) — States CANNOT secede (Art. 1)
Art. 3 bill: Simple Majority + President's permission | State opinion NOT binding
1st state on linguistic basis: Andhra Pradesh — 1 Oct 1953 (Potti Sriramulu)
States Reorganisation Act 1956 → 14 States + 6 UTs (1 Nov 1956)
Citizenship: Acquired 5 ways | Lost 3 ways | No dual citizenship (Art. 9)
⭐ CAA 2019: 3 countries, 6 religions, 6 years residency (from 11); till 31 Dec 2014
OCI (2005): NOT citizens | No voting | PIO merged with OCI in 2015
NRI: Indian passport | >182 days outside | CAN vote | NRI Day: 9 January
05
Fundamental Rights
Part III — Arts. 12 to 35 | Magna Carta of India
Originally 7 FRs → Now 6 FRs (Right to Property removed by 44th CAA 1978)
⭐ Golden Trinity: Arts. 14 + 19 + 21 | Borrowed from USA's Bill of Rights
Arts. 20 & 21 CANNOT be suspended even during National Emergency
Art. 21A (86th CAA 2002): Free education for children aged 6–14 years
Art. 32 = "Heart & Soul" (Ambedkar) | Art. 226 = HC writs (FRs + legal rights)
5 Writs: Habeas Corpus | Mandamus | Prohibition | Certiorari | Quo Warranto
Mandal Commission (1979) → 27% OBC | Indra Sawhney (1992) → 50% cap
Maneka Gandhi (1978): Art. 21 wide — procedure must be fair, just & reasonable
06
DPSP & Fundamental Duties
Part IV (Arts. 36–51) + Part IVA (Art. 51A)
DPSPs borrowed from Ireland | Non-justiciable | Positive obligations on State
Ambedkar: "Novel Features" | Austin: FRs+DPSPs = "Conscience of Constitution"
3 categories: Socialistic | Gandhian | Liberal-Intellectual
⭐ Key DPSPs: Art. 39(d) = Equal pay | Art. 44 = UCC | Art. 50 = Separate Judiciary
Champakam (1951): FRs prevail | Minerva Mills (1980): Balance between FRs & DPSPs
FDs: Part IVA | Art. 51A | 11 duties | Added by 42nd CAA 1976
Based on Swaran Singh Committee | Inspired by USSR
11th duty (clause k) added by 86th CAA 2002 — education for child 6–14 yrs
07
The Union — Parliament
President · VP · PM · Parliament (Arts. 52–151)
President = 1st Citizen | De jure executive | Electoral College: Elected MPs + MLAs
⭐ 5 Pardoning Powers: Pardon | Commutation | Remission | Respite | Reprieve
Governor CANNOT pardon death sentence — only President can
VP = 2nd Citizen | Ex-officio RS Chairman | Elected by ALL MPs (incl. nominated)
PM = 3rd Citizen | De facto executive | Chairs NITI Aayog, NDC, Inter-State Council
LS: Max 550, Current 543 | RS: Max 250, Current 245 (Permanent body)
⭐ Money Bill: Only LS origin | RS: 14 days to return | No Joint Sitting for Money Bills
Zero Hour = Indian invention | Ordinance max = 6 months + 6 weeks
08
Indian Judiciary
SC + HCs + PIL + Judicial Review
SC constituted: 28 Jan 1950 | Max strength: 34 judges | Seat: Delhi
SC law binding on all courts (Art. 141) | Retired SC judge CANNOT practice in India
Jurisdictions: Original (131) | Appellate (132–134) | Advisory (143) | Writ (32)
⭐ 2nd Judges Case (1993) = Collegium born | 3rd (1998) = CJI + 4 senior judges
NJAC (99th CAA 2014) = struck down by 4th Judges Case (2015)
25 High Courts | Delhi only UT with exclusive HC (since 1966)
PIL concept: Justice Krishna Iyer (1976) | 1st PIL: Hussainara Khatoon 1979
Vishaka case (1993): Sexual harassment violates Arts. 14, 15, 16, 21
09
The States
Governor · CM · Vidhan Sabha · Vidhan Parishad
Governor: Appointed by President | Term 5 yrs (pleasure of President) | Oath: CJ of HC
Governor CANNOT pardon death sentence or martial law sentences
CM appointed by Governor | CoM collectively responsible to Vidhan Sabha
⭐ 6 Bicameral States: K A B U T M (Karnataka, AP, Bihar, UP, Telangana, Maharashtra)
Vidhan Sabha: Max 500, Min 60 | 5-year term | MLA min age: 25
Vidhan Parishad: Max 1/3rd of LA | Min 40 | Permanent body | MLC min age: 30
LC delay: Max 4 months (3+1) | NO Joint Sitting in State Legislature
Quorum = 10% of total membership | Advocate General: appointed by Governor
10
Union Territories
Part VIII — Arts. 239–242 | 8 UTs
UTs governed by President through administrator (Art. 239)
7th CAA 1956 created UT category | Currently 8 Union Territories
⭐ 5 LTs: Delhi, Puducherry, J&K, A&N Islands, Ladakh | 3 Admins: Chandigarh, Lakshadweep, D&NH&D&D
Art. 239AA (69th CAA 1991): Delhi = NCT | 70 seats | CM by President
Delhi excluded: Public Order, Police, Land (under Centre/LG)
2018 SC ruling: LG bound by aid & advice of elected Delhi CoM
Art. 239AB: Failure of machinery in Delhi = analogous to Art. 356 (President's Rule)
Only Delhi has exclusive HC among all UTs | Chandigarh → Punjab & Haryana HC
11
Local Governance
73rd & 74th CAA — Panchayats & Municipalities
1st to recommend 3-tier PR: Balwant Rai Mehta (1957)
1st state: Rajasthan — Oct 2, 1959 (Nagaur) | PM Nehru inaugurated
⭐ 73rd CAA: 24 April 1993 | Part IX | Arts. 243–243O | 11th Schedule (29 items)
Women reservation: ≥ 1/3rd seats + Chairpersons (all levels)
State EC: Appointed by Governor | Same SFC for both Panchayats & Municipalities
⭐ 74th CAA: 1 June 1993 | Part IXA | Arts. 243P–243ZG | 12th Schedule (18 items)
3 types ULBs: Nagar Panchayat | Municipal Council | Municipal Corporation (Mayor)
Father of LSG: Lord Ripon | 1st Municipal Corp: Madras (1687)
12
Constitutional Bodies
ECI · UPSC · CAG · Finance Comm. · Lokpal
ECI (Arts. 324–329): Appointed by President | Tenure: 6 yrs or 65 | 1st CEC: Sukumar Sen
ECI: Conducts LS/RS/LA/Pres/VP elections — NOT Panchayat/Municipality
61st CAA 1988: Voting age 21→18 | NOTA: 2013 | VVPAT: Nagaland (Noksen) 2013
UPSC: Watchdog of Merit | Tenure 6 yrs or 65 | SPSC removed by President
⭐ Finance Commission: Art. 280 | Every 5 yrs | Quasi-Judicial | 16th FC: Arvind Panagariya
CAG: "Guardian of Public Purse" | Ambedkar: "Most important officer" | No re-appointment
NITI Aayog (2015): Replaced Planning Commission | PM chairs | Think Tank
Lokpal Act 2013: 1st Chair: Justice Pinaki Chandra Ghose | 5 yrs or 70 yrs
13
Constitutional Amendments
Art. 368 — 106 CAAs | 3 Types
Amendment borrowed from South Africa | Art. 368 | Neither flexible nor rigid
Type A: Simple Majority (outside Art. 368) | Type B: Special Majority | Type C: Special + ½ States
NO Joint Sitting for CAA | President MUST give assent (24th CAA 1971)
⭐ 42nd CAA 1976: "Mini Constitution" | Added Socialist, Secular, Integrity to Preamble
44th CAA 1978: Armed Rebellion | Arts. 20&21 cannot be suspended during NE
52nd CAA 1985: Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule)
86th CAA 2002: Art. 21A (education 6–14 yrs) | 101st CAA 2016: GST
106th CAA 2023: Women's Reservation — 1/3rd seats in LS & State Assemblies
14
Centre–State Relations
Legislative · Administrative · Financial | Parts XI & XII
⭐ 7th Schedule: Union (~98) | State (~61) | Concurrent (~52) | Residuary → Parliament
Art. 254: Conflict on Concurrent List → Centre prevails
5 Conditions for Parliament on State List: RS Res (249) | NE (250) | State Request (252) | Treaty (253) | PR Rule (356)
Art. 249: RS resolution by 2/3rd → 1 year validity
AIS: IAS, IPS, IFS | Ultimate control: Centre | Day-to-day: State
Art. 271: Surcharges → 100% retained by Centre (not shared)
Art. 275: Statutory (obligatory) grants | Art. 282: Discretionary grants
Sarkaria Commission (1983) → Led to Inter-State Council (1990) | Chair: PM
